segment-text: Adapted from the novel by
Written by Simon Blackwell & Armando Iannucci
JUNE - AUGUST 2018
COPYRIGHT COPPERFIELD FILMS LTD.
INT. LONDON THEATRE - EARLY EVENING

A buzzing, busy theatre. So busy that some PEOPLE stand in the aisles. DAVID, dressed smartly, walks onto a stage to applause. He goes to a reading desk, carrying a book. On the spine is: "The Personal History of David Copperfield", obscured by DAVID's hands. He's nervous, never done this before. Takes a quick, deep breath, for confidence. Puts the book on the desk. Opens it. The words on the page look fuzzy.
Whether I turn out to be the hero of my own story... The words on the page look clearer now.
(more confident) ...or whether that station will be held by anybody else... On the backcloth, the outline of some buildings on the
horizon: THE ROOKERY and a CHURCH.
...these moments must show.
DAVID wipes across screen.
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE - SALTMARSH - EVENING

We are suddenly in the middle of flat, Norfolk countryside. YOUNG HAM runs ahead. Some of the theatre still physically present in the field: lights, the front row of audience, part of the red boxes and seats. DAVID turns and walks towards the horizon. Shape of THE ROOKERY (David's Childhood Home) and a CHURCH in silhouette on the horizon. Loud sea and heavy winds heard in the distance. Swift intercutting, with build-up of music, of the following (with the occasional O.S. YELP of CLARA COPPERFIELD):
EXT. YARMOUTH - KINGS STAITHE LANE - EVENING
A man in his mid-50s - PEGGOTTY's older brother, DANIEL PEGGOTTY, whizzes past on his HORSE-DRAWN CART.
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE - SALTMASH - EVENING
DR CHILLIP runs across a field.
EXT./INT. ROOKERY - EVENING
PEGGOTTY runs out of The Rookery, and then into a back-room.
I'll be three seconds! Two! Don't fret! Peggotty's still here! One second!
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE - HORIZON - EVENING
YOUNG HAM, a boy of about nine, running, carrying a bucket.
EXT. NORFOLK - COUNTRY ROAD - EVENING
DANIEL PEGGOTTY's carriage riding down a path.
(to himself) Keep calm, Daniel. Be quick but keep calm.
EXT. ROOKERY - EVENING
DAVID walking into The Rookery gardens, through an open gate.
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE - EVENING
The figure of BETSEY TROTWOOD appearing over the horizon.
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE - HORIZON - EVENING
YOUNG HAM now running back followed by a NURSE.
INT. ROOKERY - EVENING
PEGGOTTY running through the house, carrying towels.
Here come the towels! And here
comes the baby! Oh my Lord!
EXT. ROOKERY - EVENING
DAVID now nearly at the house. MUSIC increases, a sense of ticking time. Running. Feet. Behind, mid-shots. Close on trundling wheels of DANIEL PEGGOTTY'S carriage.
EXT. NORFOLK - COUNTRY ROAD - EVENING
YOUNG HAM and the NURSE running towards DANIEL's carriage, getting on board.
Up you get. Mind your shins.
EXT. ROOKERY - EVENING
BETSEY getting closer to The Rookery, larger in frame. We travel with her, towards the house.
INT. ROOKERY - KITCHEN - EVENING

All intercut with shots of a grandfather clock with pendulum, getting closer to midnight. CLARA, heavily pregnant, doubling up in pain. Camera attached to the clock's pendulum as it moves back and forth, showing the Rookery interior swinging rhythmically.
INT. ROOKERY - FRONT ROOM - EVENING
DAVID steps into the Rookery.
To begin my life.....
A yelp of pain from CLARA, DAVID's mother, who is bent over.
...with the beginning of my life.
BETSEY framed in the window, approaching fast. Not stopping, she presses her nose to it with a BUMP! Everyone jumps. BETSEY, with flat white nose, slowly looks about. Sees CLARA.
(muffled by the window) Mrs David Copperfield, I think? (glass steamed via breath) Miss Trotwood. You've heard of her?
Yes, I've had that pleasure.
PEGGOTTY opens the door. BETSEY steps sideways from the steamed window, into the door frame.
Well now you see her.

Flustered, CLARA makes a meaningless half-bow half-curtsey, and sits down. BETSEY enters, hangs up her bonnet. Lopsided. Straightens it. Does it again. As she speaks, she shifts various hats, coats and umbrellas into a more symmetrical position. Notices name-plate by the door: THE ROOKERY.
In the name of Heaven, why Rookery?
When my husband bought the house, he liked to think there were rooks about it.
BETSEY glances about her, as if the rooks might lie in wait.
And are there?
(contraction) ...Noooooooooo!
(constantly moving things) My brother all over! Calls a house a rookery and takes the rooks on trust! A better name would be
"Gullible Manor".
BETSEY sits down. PEGGOTTY's had enough. She steps between BETSEY and CLARA.
(to CLARA, eye-balling
Will this... person be stopping, Ma'am?
Now, Peggotty...
(incredulously) Peggotty? You mean to say a human being has gone into a church and got herself named 'Peggotty'?
Moves a china ornament - a fisherman - a fraction of an inch.
Did your mother sneeze your name when you were being christened?
It's a normal name. And do you not think 'Trotwood' is a big glass house to be chucking stones from?
PEGGOTTY moves the ornament a fraction of an inch back.
(in pain) Aaargh!
PEGGOTTY hurries to CLARA. BETSEY moves the fisherman again.
Aha! The girl! Here comes the girl!
PEGGOTTY gets CLARA to her feet.
Or it could be... (mid-contraction, very low, guttural) ...a boooooooooy...
PEGGOTTY and BETSEY taken aback by this noise.

It's certain to be a girl. And I beg you to call her Betsey Trotwood Copperfield and have me as her godmother. (gets up, points at CLARA's bump) There must be no mistakes in life with this Betsey Trotwood. There must be no trifling with her affections, poor dear.
CLARA yelps. BETSEY takes a good look at her face, moving CLARA's hair away from her eyes.
Why bless my soul, you're so young. PEGGOTTY can't support CLARA alone. Ham!
'Ham'? No no. Hot water. She's birthing, not dining.
YOUNG HAM appears at her elbow.
(to BETSEY) I'm Ham. Ma'am. It's my nam...name. Lunacy.
Get the nurse and the doctor!
DR CHILLIP and the NURSE arrive.
We're here!
They help CLARA upstairs. Screams.
Let's get you upstairs. Hurry!
Yeeees! Hurry! (O.S.)

BETSEY sits and produces a large package of jeweller's cotton. She inserts a strand in either ear. The chair's between two potted plants, one a big geranium. But not exactly mid-way. BETSEY shifts her chair until it is. A shot of BETSEY from the PENDULUM's POV.
INT. ROOKERY - FOOT OF STAIRS - NIGHT
A beat of BETSEY's POV: ears stuffed, the panic in silence as people run up and down the stairs, fetching water. She fiddles with her BROACH. The clock, approaching midnight. HAM has gone.
(hidden away in a corner) ...I record that I was born on a Friday, at twelve o'clock at night.
PEGGOTTY runs down signalling to BETSEY. BETSEY uncorks her ears. We hear mayhem, shouts, and a BABY. The clock chiming midnight, in perfect rhythm to the BABY DAVID's cries.
(getting up) How is she?
She starts walking towards PEGGOTTY, who legs it back upstairs as DR CHILLIP, flushed, comes down.
As comfortable as we can expect a young mother to be.
No - she. How is she? The baby?
It's a boy, ma'am. I'm happy to
(walking towards CHILLIP) Why congratulate? Is the boy the first of twins with his sister being born as we speak? Er...

DR CHILLIP shakes his head. BETSEY grabs her bonnet and looks like she may hit DR CHILLIP hard with it. Thinks better of it off PEGGOTTY's look, exits, still with one long piece of cotton dangling from her ear. PEGGOTTY reappears and, almost in one move, rearranges the coats etc as they were.
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE - HORIZON - NIGHT
WIDE on BETSEY walking off, at speed.
INT. ROOKERY - BABY DAVID'S POV - DAY

Sounds of the BABY DAVID gurgling. Screen fills with light. Gradually, indistinct shapes appear. Over these, we can, on occasion, vaguely see DAVID and BABY DAVID's hands, and the edge of a crib. Suddenly CLARA's face comes in close, blowing a raspberry.
Look at you, Baby Davy. Face like a peach. I'm very fond of peaches. She playfully leans in, as if to eat him. We briefly see DAVID:
I remember Peggotty's rough fingers, like a pocket nutmeggrater...
C/U of PEGGOTTY's huge coarse fingers.
EXT. ROOKERY - YARD - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

A flow of images from FOUR-YEAR-OLD DAVID's POV; indistinct, fuzzy. Trees, a garden, a box with St Paul's on the lid. FOUR-YEAR-OLD DAVID peeks through the spindly legs of hugely tall hens. A strange hedge. FOUR-YEAR-OLD DAVID fighting it with an oversized toy sword.
INT/EXT. ROOKERY - LANDING/CHURCHYARD - DAY
DAVID inside, at the window. From his POV we see the church, and distant gravestones. Trees seem to bend over it, like giants. As he speaks, FOUR-YEAR-OLD DAVID joins him.
I see my father's gravestone,
shadowed by trees bending to one another in the wind, like giants
INT. ROOKERY - PARLOUR - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
The lights dim, a winter fire in the parlour. FOUR-YEAR-OLD DAVID, and PEGGOTTY in a corner of the room. FOUR-YEAR-OLD DAVID (reading to PEGGOTTY)
A CROCODILE scuttles out the door; stop-frame paper/wooden animation.
What a remarkable vegetable.
(laughing) Not vegetable! Reptile!
So I said. One of them. What a world of gammon and spinnage it is!
Stood by the window, DAVID looks to CLARA, who sits now with YOUNG DAVID, and PEGGOTTY.
(to CLARA) A world of gammon and spinnage!
CLARA writes it down. YOUNG DAVID copies her.
INT. ROOKERY - PANTRY - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
The figure of MURDSTONE approaches. PEGGOTTY picks up a basket of washing.
The gentleman is here, ma'am, with the black hair and particular manner, who walked home with you from church last Sunday.
MURDSTONE comes through the doorway, a big towering figure, carrying a RIDING CROP. CLARA immediately delighted.
(re a plant by the door, pulling a bit off) Now, is this your famous geranium? (spotting DAVID) Ah, and you must be the man of the house?
I am a boy, sir.
YOUNG DAVID holds on to CLARA, with his right hand.
Dear boy. Come! Shake hands!
MURDSTONE has thick black hair in his ears. His hand, with signet ring, looms huge. YOUNG DAVID goes to shake it, but with his left hand.
(loud, mocking) That's the wrong hand, boy!
YOUNG DAVID sticks with his left hand. Extends it even
(drops his hand, with a look to CLARA) Maybe your way will catch on. You're a brave fellow.

YOUNG DAVID stares at MURDSTONE's hand. CLARA gives PEGGOTTY a conspiratorial nod. PEGGOTTY understands. She still holds the washing basket. Putting it down, we now see it's a CRAN full of HERRING. She takes YOUNG DAVID's hand.
Davy, my sweet little pudden, let me take you to Yarmouth. My brother can drive us...
A SAILOR walks by with a LARGE FISH.
"Yar-muth?" The walls of the house fall, like tarpaulin, that is picked
EXT. YARMOUTH HARBOUR SIDE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
A 16-year-old young woman - EMILY - is among a group of other WOMEN standing at a long workbench, gutting herring. Hard, messy work. EMILY seems broken down by her tough job.
EXT. YARMOUTH ROAD - DAY
A cart, driven by DANIEL PEGGOTTY. YOUNG DAVID and PEGGOTTY are beside him surrounded by the flat NORFOLK LANDSCAPE.
Look at all that sky Davy boy! Too much sky even for a bird.
(wriggling, never still) If the world is really as round as my geography book says it is, how can this bit of it be so flat?
It's not to your liking, Davy?
I certainly think it might be improved by a small hill.
They drive over a small bump.
That do you?
EXT. YARMOUTH HARBOUR SIDE - DAY
We're into town. A busy harbour. As HAM arrives, now a young man in his late teens, EMILY pulls off her apron.
It's 4. I'm done gutting.
She walks off. A few moments later, DANIEL PEGGOTTY's cart pulls up beside HAM.
(Leaps off, hugs HAM) My Ham! I turn my back and you sprout like a beanstalk!
I'd forgotten how hard you squeeze, Peggotty. You'll have the marrow out my bones.
She's a human mangle that woman. Master Davy, this is Ham. Ham, Master Davy!
Pleased to meet you, Ham. Likewise.
Is Emily here?
No, it's 4HAM (CONT'D) -She's done gutting.
Ah, she's done gutting.
EXT. YARMOUTH - DEVIL'S ALLEY - DAY
All smiles - HAM, with YOUNG DAVID on his back, walks PEGGOTTY, DANIEL PEGGOTTY and the luggage under an archway, down a little lane, and onto...
EXT. YARMOUTH CLIFFTOP / BEACH - DAY
Vast blue skies and flat shingle, sharp in the Norfolk light.
That's where we all live, Davy.
DANIEL PEGGOTTY points to an UPTURNED BOAT on the beach. Smoke comes from a funnel in the roof. A couple more BOATHOUSES sit further up the beach.
Look at that. It's no mansion.
It's a downside-upside capsized boat.
YOUNG DAVID grins, jumps down.
It's Aladdin's Palace! We'll be like spiders trapped under a teacup!
He runs towards the boathouse.
Digs for joy that boy, finds it too.
But can he pick crabs out a bucket without losing a finger?
INT. BOATHOUSE - DAY
Feels enormous. Table, framed biblical scenes. From a former bench hangs an oil lamp. Boat's wheel is a clothes airer. Davy...
PEGGOTTY pulls across a curtain and reveals a small whitewashed room with a bed, a little window, a mirror framed with oyster-shells. Clean, bright, perfect.
Peggotty! This is the most desirable bedroom I've ever seen!
Desirable! I love your words Davy.
DANIEL PEGGOTTY is behind them with EMILY and HAM.
(to YOUNG DAVID) Peggotty says your mother's a lady.
Emily! Too bold by half.
(aside, to DANIEL PEGGOTTY) Are Ham and Emily your children?
Adopted. Both their fathers were drownded.
In an armchair in the corner, knitting, sits MRS GUMMIDGE.
(re DAVID) Oh, Ham! Not another mouth to feed! Let me die and be a riddance!
Come now Mrs G, your funeral would be a far greater nuisance than an extra place at the table!
(to PEGGOTTY, re MRS G) Is she upset?
(quietly) That's Mrs Gummidge. Her husband was drownded too.
PEGGOTTY hands MRS GUMMIDGE fish and potatoes.
How's that then? You can't complain about a nice bit of kipper.
You just watch her.
The potatoes are burnt like coals...
Here she goes...
These taters could be my last.
Can I go out on the beach, uncle?
You done your gutting?
Yes, I've done my gutting.
There's a lot of gutting to do... Fish go off, you know.
EMILY shoots him a 'thanks for nothing' look.
I've been gutting fish since dawn.
Go on then. But take young Davy with ya.
Quickly, mind, we should be getting on with the next batch.
(as they leave) What's gutting?
EXT. FENLAND - DAY.
Wide on YOUNG DAVID and EMILY. YOUNG DAVID picking up bits of ferns/a stick.
Is your mother really a lady?
Yeah, I think so.
Does she attend to her correspondence and receive callers in the drawing room?
I don't know.. A gentleman with big hands calls to admire our geranium.
I should like to become a lady.
He had two eyebrows. I say eyebrows, rather than eyes, because they're much more important in his face.
(what?) Yes...
EXT. YARMOUTH BEACH - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
YOUNG DAVID and EMILY leave the boathouse together. Later. EMILY skimming stones across the water's surface, expertly. YOUNG DAVID impressed, excited, nervous.
I like the seaside very much.
The sea is cruel and brutish. I've seen it tear a boat as big as our house all to pieces.
She skims a stone. YOUNG DAVID, more awkward, carries on.
I hope it wasn't the boat that your father was drownded in?
'Drowned'. Uncle says 'drownded' and he's wrong. It's a silly, Yarmouth way of speaking. The word is drowned.
(gabbling now) I never saw my father. He's... normal dead. My mother and I and Peggotty are by ourselves. But in the happiest state imaginable.
Tries to skim a stone. He's rubbish, ends up accidentally flinging it behind him instead of out to sea. He attempts to skim again. Terrible - it hits the shingle, doesn't even make the water.
EXT. YARMOUTH HARBOUR SIDE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
A busy harbour, boats creaking in the high wind. MEN unload fish. WOMEN work at the long workbench, gutting herring. YOUNG DAVID and EMILY walk along.
Seems a fair life, to work on a boat, or in the harbour.
Your hands get red raw and you can't ever - ever - escape the smell.
(distracted by the boats) Your hands... have nice skin.
Then suddenly...
Look! Look at this, Davy!
EMILY is already climbing up a mast, near to the top. If she falls she'll be crushed between boats.
You can see past Yarmouth.
From EMILY's height, we see the FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRYSIDE. HAM is below, concerned and angry.
Come down! You'll smash in twenty pieces if you slip off there.
I'm not scared!
I know. But come down! Uncle and Peggotty are asking for us.
EXT. BOATHOUSE - DAY
YOUNG DAVID with HAM and EMILY, crossing the shingle.
You won't mention the mast, will you?
No, I won't mention it.
It was very high.
It's not high. Nowhere is high around here.
INT. BOATHOUSE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
YOUNG DAVID enters with HAM and EMILY to a cheer. DANIEL and PEGGOTTY grinning. Cakes and beer - a celebration.
(beaming, to EMILY) I told them. What we decided between us. Our 'news'.
Getting engaged.
Getting engaged, yeah.
Just say getting engaged, Ham.
We're uh, we're engaged.
Are you happy for us, uncle?
Happy? I'm happy as a dog with two bones!
They think he's finished. PEGGOTTY is about to speak butDANIEL PEGGOTTY (CONT'D)
PEGGOTTY rushes past and hugs HAM.
You two engaged to be married. Oh Lord, I'm going to cry.
She hugs HAM. Then PEGGOTTY starts dancing with YOUNG DAVID, and HAM starts dancing with EMILY.
Everybody should get married!
C/U on smiling YOUNG DAVID, smiling PEGGOTTY, smiling DANIEL PEGGOTTY, smiling EMILY, smiling HAM. Then, MRS GUMMIDGE:
Let me die, as a favour to myself.

Suddenly: A SHADOW looming, and then RUMBLING from the roof of the boathouse. It begins to shake. Looking up, we see the wood splintering, the boathouse beginning to tear and split. Everyone watches, debris falling on them. DAYLIGHT shines through the hole. Close on YOUNG DAVID. The roof now seems to be made partly of paper. Some MASSIVE FINGERS come in through the hole. The characters now appear to be frozen in happy party-mode as life-sized drawings, but in their clothes. Some bits of paper fall around them. The boathouse is made entirely of paper. The full HAND (with SIGNET RING) of MURDSTONE coming through the roof. The lifesize paper people now appear to have been drawn by a child.
Hello, what have you got there?
INT. ROOKERY - LANDING / KITCHEN - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

From YOUNG DAVID's POV, MURDSTONE reaches down and picks up the drawing. YOUNG DAVID, on the floor with his toys and books, has been drawing boathouses. On other pieces of paper he's written words and phrases he heard in Yarmouth.
A house made from a boat? Draw a boat, or draw a house, none of this nonsense!
CLARA behind MURDSTONE: weaker, sheepish.
(to CLARA) Mrs Copperfield - is it all...? Is that a new ring or your proper one?
(unconvincing happiness) Yes! You must congratulate me! PEGGOTTY does not do so.
You've got a Pa, David! A new one.
Astonished and upset, YOUNG DAVID glances round for him. A new Pa?
Your mother and I are now married.
I meant to tell you sooner Davy...

YOUNG DAVID glances out the window to the churchyard - his expression changes as, a moment later, the CHURCH and his FATHER'S GRAVESTONE are next to the window, as if looking in. PEGGOTTY goes towards YOUNG DAVID, MURDSTONE blocks.
(sotto, to PEGGOTTY) You addressed my wife by a surname that is not hers. She is now Mrs Murdstone. Will you remember that?

PEGGOTTY gives a curtsey of loathing as we hear the front door, and they head downstairs: MISS JANE MURDSTONE, Murdstone's sister, has arrived. PEGGOTTY helps a COACHMAN with the luggage: black boxes, with the initials JM on the lids in brass nails. JANE pays out of a hard steel purse that clicks loudly.
My sister, Jane Murdstone. My wife, Clara Murdstone.
PEGGOTTY hushes YOUNG DAVID before he speaks out...
A fair choice. I regret I missed the wedding, and the chance to meet you at the peak of your beauty.
CLARA curtseys... waits for MURDSTONE to introduce Davy, who watches her from the foot of the stairs. He does not. CLARA pushes DAVID forward.
A boy? I presume it is named?
I am David, Miss Murdstone. Pleased to meet you.
(fake-smile) My question was not directed at you, child. (to MURDSTONE, no smile) Wants manners.
She picks up the china fisherman, looks at it, and then to MURDSTONE. Puts it back down. CLARA hovers; a look of "told you so" from PEGGOTTY.
Can I help you at all, Miss? No.
(to CLARA) If you'll be so good as to give me your keys, my dear.
CLARA gets them from her purse. Gives them to MISS MURDSTONE, who moves off. PEGGOTTY, aghast, follows. CLARA embraces YOUNG DAVID.
Please, David. Love your new father and be obedient to him.
Why are you whispering and saying this so hurriedly and secretly, as if it's wrong?
She puts her hand in his, and leads him into the parlour, their hands behind YOUNG DAVID's back so as not to be seen.
INT. ROOKERY - PARLOUR - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
JANE opens and shuts cupboards, using the keys. Sniffs one. Doesn't like it. PEGGOTTY trailing behind her.
The parlour's rather bright.
I'll take care of it. (to PEGGOTTY) You have a man secreted somewhere about the place, do you not?
No, Madam! Who keeps a man in a cupboard? What's in it for him?
JANE makes her way back towards the china fisherman.
(starts to cry) Am I not to be consulted on decoration? In my own house...
"My own house"? Clara?
Our own house, I mean...
Tense stillness. Then a burst of energy: MISS MURDSTONE picks up her bag, drops the keys on the floor.
It's clear my status in this house is lower than I anticipated. I
shall go immediately.
Jane Murdstone, be silent!
YOUNG DAVID picks the keys up. MURDSTONE snatches them off him, without even looking down. Gives them to JANE. She puts the keys away, and her bag down.
(calm, to MISS MURDSTONE) It is not my fault so unusual an occurrence has taken place tonight.
Let us both try to forget it. Boy, up to bed this instant!
YOUNG DAVID, close to tears, is ushered out by PEGGOTTY. The door shuts on us.
INT. ROOKERY - FOOT OF STAIRS / PANTRY - DAY
A rainy morning. YOUNG DAVID and PEGGOTTY watch MISS MURDSTONE through the open door. Dressed in a black velvet gown she's still opening and slamming cupboards.
She looks like she's made of wax.
Or Dutch cheese.
Muffled laughter from them.
Is she searching for somewhere secret to sleep, so she can jump out and terrify us?
I reckon she doesn't sleep. She
just... hangs. Like a bat. He impersonates Miss Murdstone as a bat, 'wings' folded, teeth biting lower lip. PEGGOTTY stifles a laugh.
(as the "bat")
"I presume it is named?" Suddenly MR MURDSTONE is behind them. CLARA smiling beside him.
Davy, boy. Time for your lesson.
INT. ROOKERY - PARLOUR / STAIRWELL - DAY
CLARA reading at her desk, MURDSTONE in an armchair by the window, MISS MURDSTONE stringing noisy steel beads. They stare at YOUNG DAVID, standing up reciting his lesson.
...and verbs have two voices: one, active; two... er...
CLARA closes her book, and tries to mouth the word 'passive'. CLARA!
(instant) Jane!
YOUNG DAVID focuses on the beads. CLINKING. The CLOCK ticking unbearably loudly.
We should switch to a less enjoyable activity. Jane!
Oh, Davy, Davy!
Don't say, "Oh, Davy, Davy." He either knows his lesson, or he does not.
He does not.
MURDSTONE goes to the bookshelf, takes the crocodile book, flings it at YOUNG DAVID's head. Dodges. It hits the floor.
Pick it up. Read it to me.

YOUNG DAVID opens it. The words look normal, but each time we see the book they're out of order, gibberish, or only a few on the page, some on the floor. YOUNG DAVID can't speak. Looks at MURDSTONE, who has a stray letter on his face.
Sorry, sir. The words have skates on and skim away. I'm very stupid.
He looks to CLARA. She shakes her head.
You'd soon as teach the furniture.
Jane Murdstone, silence!
Not 'stupid', perhaps, justMURDSTONE Clara Murdstone, silence!
(innocent, instinctively) Clara Copperfield, Sir!
A terrible hush. MURDSTONE takes a cane from the bookshelf.
Edward! No, please... Clara!
MURDSTONE
YOUNG DAVID backs up to a wall, cornered by MURDSTONE. Taking YOUNG DAVID's arm, MURDSTONE leads him towards the door. CLARA runs towards them. MISS MURDSTONE stops her.
Let your husband improve your son!

CLARA grapples for YOUNG DAVID's hand, blocked by JANE. MURDSTONE pulls back - on YOUNG DAVID's other arm. YOUNG DAVID violently pulled in both directions. CLARA lets go. Murdstone drags YOUNG DAVID upstairs, stepping on and destroying the paper/wooden crocodile we saw earlier.
If I have an obstinate horse or dog to deal with, I beat him.
INT. ROOKERY - DAVID'S BEDROOM / STAIRWELL / LANDING - DAY40
MURDSTONE shuts the door, drops his cane and puts YOUNG DAVID in a headlock.
I conquer him, even if it costs him all the blood he has.
I've tried to learn sir, but I can't when you and Miss Murdstone watch me.
Can't you indeed?
From within the headlock, YOUNG DAVID bites down hard on MURDSTONE's hand/wrist.
(pathetic yelp) Aagh!

MURDSTONE stumbles back. A pathetic yelp as he hits his head on a cupboard. He pushes YOUNG DAVID, and YOUNG DAVID pushes back. YOUNG DAVID scuttles under the bed. MURDSTONE lunges for his cane and follows YOUNG DAVID, but can't reach. YOUNG DAVID kicks a chamber pot in MURDSTONE's direction. MURDSTONE rears up with the bed on his back. Scrambling out, YOUNG DAVID jumps on the bed. MURDSTONE grabs YOUNG DAVID, throws him to the floor and starts caning him. CLARA, PEGGOTTY and JANE are on the Landing.
Edward! Please stop!
Clara, enough!
Let me break down the door, Mrs Copperfield!
Mrs Murdstone! Jane!
Edward is teaching. Let him teach.
The beating continues. YOUNG DAVID on the floor, curled up.
INT. ROOKERY - DAVID'S BEDROOM - NIGHT

It's dark now, other than a small candle. YOUNG DAVID lies in the same position. He's scribbling on a piece of paper: "Conquer him.' Downstairs, a muffled conversation between the MURDSTONES, with CLARA weeping. Then a nearer voice...
(whispered) Davy.
YOUNG DAVID kneels by the keyhole with a candle. Light leaks in beneath the door.
INT. ROOKERY - LANDING / DAVID'S BEDROOM - NIGHT
PEGGOTTY's side of the door is well-lit. In some shots we see both sides at once, as a WIDE.
What's to become of us, Peggotty?
YOUNG DAVID has kept his mouth to the hole, not his ear to
My brother will... Pardon?
He puts his ear to the keyhole. But so does Peggotty.
Say again Davy? What?
Then they get back into sync.
My brother will take me in. I can look after Mrs Gummidge and gut fish. Or the other way round.
What's going to be done with me?

A CLINK of keys, the light cutting out momentarily, and the door is suddenly open. A BURST of light as MISS MURDSTONE stands in the doorway. The open door lets light into the room, briefly showing that YOUNG DAVID has been writing and drawing over pieces of paper, which entirely cover the floor.
You're to be sent away. To school?
(laughs bleakly) 'To school!'
YOUNG DAVID smiles, until he sees PEGGOTTY, who shakes her head. MR MURDSTONE looms behind her, his hand elaborately bandaged. We're close on him, as he says:
Education, boy, is costly.
There's the sound of a distant rumbling. A gust of wind blows out YOUNG DAVID's candle. A wisp of smoke trails in the air as YOUNG DAVID tries to gather his scraps of paper.
What is before you, is a fight with the world.

MURDSTONE glances right, and we catch a glimpse of: An openback cart blitzing towards the room, coming from darkness. An oil lamp burning. The cart barrels through the room. YOUNG DAVID's papers are sent flying.
(shouting) The sooner you begin it the better!
INT/EXT. ROOKERY / FLAT NORFOLK LANDSCAPE - NIGHT

We're outside, bits of debris and paper falling as the cart travels over us and rushes into the night. YOUNG DAVID sits beside THE DRIVER. Inside, the MURDSTONES smile from the bedroom window. JANE is playing with her steel beads: clink, clink. The further away, the louder the clinking. Close on her hand, clinking beads. CLINK! CLINK! From the side, we push in CLOSE on beads moving through her hand, on a chain...
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY

A row of bottles, chinking along a production line. MASSIVE NOISE of clinking bottles. A hot, busy warehouse. Filthy BOYS and GIRLS. Hands going in and out of machinery, grabbing hot bottles. A CHILD with one arm. Some boys wash empties at a trough. Others at workbenches pasting labels, fitting seals, packing cases. A huge structure containing countless bottles stands impressive and vulnerable. YOUNG DAVID's with an older boy, MICK WALKER, and MEALY POTATOES, YOUNG DAVID's age.
(shouting over the noise) Cork with the hand corker - yeah? (hands him a bottle) Pass it to Mealy Potatoes, he seals. Five a minute or old Creakle hangs your guts out for bunting.
They look to CREAKLE, sitting at the desk in his office. YOUNG DAVID tries to cork the bottle. Can't pull down the lever. MEALY laughs. As do some other boys.
Where you living?
I'm to lodge with the Micawber family, whom I've yet to meet.
"Whom I've yet to meet." Where was you brung up, Windsor Castle?
MICK/MEALY tosses YOUNG DAVID a bottle. CRASH! A whoop from the boys. A grubby man, TUNGAY, comes out fast.
Quiet! Quiet!
He grabs YOUNG DAVID, drags him to...
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - CREAKLE'S OFFICE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
MR CREAKLE stares out the window, his back to YOUNG DAVID. Speaks in a whisper so low some words are repeated by TUNGAY.
You know my rules. Half a day's pay per bottle...
(repeating) ...per bottle.
CREAKLE turns. He is a stout, balding, red-faced man.
Oh! The famous biting boy. Here.
...biting boy. Here.
YOUNG DAVID walks over to CREAKLE.
I have the happiness of knowing your step... (cuts in) ...step!..
...father... (cuts in) Father!
...A man of a strong character. ...aracter.
CREAKLE hands to TUNGAY a big piece of card, string attached.
Tie it to him Tungay.
...to him, Tungay. (aside) That's me. Sorry.
The placard reads 'HE BITES'. TUNGAY ties it to YOUNG DAVID's shoulders like a knapsack, pushes him into...
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
The BOYS see the placard. Start whooping and laughing again. Quiet.
(quietly) Quiet.
(strains to be louder) Quiet! (realisation) Quiet!!
The placard knocks a bottle. YOUNG DAVID catches it, but stands and knocks another: CRASH! Whoops from the WORKERS.
Look at the writing on his back! Ha! (to Mick) What's it say?
EXT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY - LATE AFTERNOON
YOUNG DAVID, minus placard, drags a trunk printed with "DC".
EXT. LONDON COACHING INN - STREET - DAY - LATE AFTERNOON Busy street. From YOUNG DAVID's height: loud traffic, horse

legs, crowds. Carriage wheels whizz past like fast cars. He crosses the dangerous road, pulling his trunk. He has a piece of paper with an address. Looks around. One side of the Athenaeum looks safe; a clean open road. YOUNG DAVID shows a MAN the paper, and is sent towards a dark, scuzzy, scary alleyway (Long Lane). Dodgy-looking MEN lurk around the entrance.
EXT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - DAY (CONTINUOUS).
YOUNG DAVID approaches. A COALMAN and BOOTMAKER bang the door.
Swindler! Open the door!
Pay up, you weasel! Pay your debts!
Suddenly, a VOICE calls from a dark alleyway next to him:
Hsst! Are you Master Copperfield?
Er... yes. Is that... Mr Micawber?
MR MICAWBER peers out from behind a water butt.
Master Copperfield, it would be of material assistance to me if you'd join those gentlemen, echo their slanderous cries, and then enunciate the following: "Ere!
Round the back! 'E's flitting!"
(practicing) '...Here.'
''Ere'. As in the aural organ.
'Round the back. He's...' Flitting.
'Flitting'.
Precisely! Splendid. Well, no time like the present!
Gives YOUNG DAVID a friendly shove. He approaches the MEN.
Yes... pay up please. Pay the money or else...I'll be out of pocket...
MICAWBER makes an encouraging gesture: 'Now!'
'Ere! Round the back! He's... The word's gone. But the BOOTMAKER is staring at him.
What, scarpering?
The word comes back to him.
...Flitting!

With a roar they charge down the lane. MICAWBER races out from his hiding place to the front door. But the BOOTMAKER sees MICAWBER and he and the others roar past YOUNG DAVID to thump on the door, which slams shut just in time. Suddenly MICAWBER opens the window, grabs YOUNG DAVID, hoiks him in, slams the shutters closed. After a pause, the shutters are opened again, and MICAWBER and YOUNG DAVID lean over and pull the trunk in too.
INT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
YOUNG DAVID a bit shocked at being manhandled.
(tense, attempting normal) A bravura performance Master Copperfield! Welcome to our home!
Banging on the window. YOUNG DAVID shakes MICAWBER's hand.
Who are...?
Business acquaintances. Whom I believe may have a disagreement with the previous tenant.
Pay up Micawber!
MRS MICAWBER emerges. She shakes YOUNG DAVID's hand.
Jackals, is what they are! Hyenas! A pleasure to make your acquaintance.

A sparsely furnished room. MRS MICAWBER now feeding one of her BABY TWINS. The other is in a cot. There are two other children - a young BOY and a GIRL of three. Throughout the film we're never sure how many CHILDREN the MICAWBERS have.
This woman is the apple of my eye, Master Copperfield, the lodestar upon whom the sextant of my heart is set... in short, my wife.
YOUNG DAVID bows to her.
How do you do.
A face at the living room window - a new CREDITOR.
I'm owed for candles! Pay me!

At another window, the COALMAN's hand reaches in for a carriage clock on a table. MICAWBER grabs the clock, puts it on a dresser, struggles with the hand, closes the window. Pull out to see a smaller window near the dresser. A HAND comes in, grabs the clock.
Right! That's it. This is too much.
I shall end it. Where's my razor?! He hands the BABY to MRS MICAWBER.
(miming a razor to throat) Swift! Final! Let them have their blood!
Never! If you are to exit, then so am I!
She in turn hands the BABY to DAVID. They hug, emotional, dramatic. The TODDLERS run in to join the hug. YOUNG DAVID confused. The BOY takes the BABY off him.
If it would help, I have some money from Mr Murdstone for my supper.
Half a smile from MICAWBER. They all look starving. Even the BABY seems to look at DAVID with wider eyes in anticipation.
INT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - LIVING ROOM - EVENING
MR MICAWBER and the CHILDREN are with DAVID, sitting at a table, napkins tucked into their collars.
Would you have your concertina about you, Wilkins? He has a gift, Master Copperfield.

MR MICAWBER reaches into his bag and produces a concertina, puts his fingers in place, readies himself. A beat. Then produces the worst music anyone has ever made: a nightmarish rendition of Auld Lang Syne.
(heading to the kitchen) Angels in his fingertips!
MRS MICAWBER comes out with a dish of potatoes and chops. She pours beer from a jug into a glass, in front of MR MICAWBER. He stops playing and looks at the beer, frowning.
Is something wrong, Mr Micawber?
Cloudy. Some individuals whose
have not as yet been extensive - in short, those who are new to London can find the local ale upsetting to the point of nausea. (staring at the ale) I could try it, if you like?
Only if it may be consumed safely.
I don't think it'll hurt me if I throw my head back and take it off quick.
He takes a huge gulp. He's fine.
There you go.
I think it's quite safe.
I am happy for the remainder to take the same route.
MICAWBER nods his thanks, downs the rest. He eyes YOUNG DAVID's plate.
Ah, Mrs Micawber is renowned for
her way with a mutton chop.
Would you care for one?
Oh no no. They are your particular chops and your specific taters. There is nourishment enough for us in honest cabbage leaves.
You would be very welcome. This is

like a royal banquet. MR MICAWBER takes a chop by the bone and a potato, eats them. The two KIDS spot this, take a chop each and some potatoes, until all that's left is a tiny scrap. YOUNG DAVID eats this.
INT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - A VERY VERY SMALL ROOM - NIGHT
YOUNG DAVID puts his box down on a hard floor. There's a double bed frame, but no mattress.
Did you sell the bedding?
MR and MRS MICAWBER appear.
I believe we may have, temporarily, liquidated the capital. In the meantime, you may take the sofa.
We exchanged it for some spoons.
Then you shall spend the night on my bed, our two dining chairs. I have, in any case, very little use for sleep.
It's true. He simply can't rest. Mr Micawber's mind is a machine of
perpetual motion. MICAWBER gravely acknowledges the truth of this.
INT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - A VERY VERY SMALL ROOM - NIGHT
YOUNG DAVID lies across two tatty dining chairs at the centre of the empty bed frame, covered with an overcoat. He looks at his "He Bites" sign and starts scribbling on the back of it.
(mimicking MICAWBER) "They are your particular chops and your specific 'taters."
EXT. STREET OUTSIDE MICAWBER'S HOUSE - DAY
MR MICAWBER leads YOUNG DAVID out of an alley, to work. A carriage passes.

London - fuller of wonders and wickedness than all the cities on earth. And it's ours, to go wherever we choose. (reads colour-coded map) But not down there. Creditors make that road impassable. A baker and a cook's shop. They take a different route.
EXT. LONDON STREETS - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

We now see the grand, exciting London David only caught glimpses of before: the city is confident. Cranes and building everywhere. MICAWBER and YOUNG DAVID round a corner, and run towards us. MICAWBER concluding a speech to YOUNG DAVID.
..annual expenditure twenty pounds nought and six, result - misery! We are still pursued...! Behind them come more CREDITORS. We keep up with MICAWBER and
YOUNG DAVID. Then they do an about-turn.
Two tailors and a most unreasonable muffin man.
They cross the road, walking in unison behind carriages and carts to stay hidden from view.
You find us fallen back financially but something shall turn up.
Won't you run out of roads?
EXT. LONDON - MARKET - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

One of the market stalls has pig carcasses on hooks. YOUNG DAVID on MICAWBER's shoulders wearing MICAWBER's hat. They duck behind a carcass as it slides along the rack. From one angle they're a mad pig-boy-man hybrid.
I know you! You come here!
MICAWBER starts to run. As does YOUNG DAVID.
EXT. LONDON - EXCHANGE ALLEY - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
MICAWBER and YOUNG DAVID chased at full pelt down a narrow alleyway. Hurtling round a corner.
St Paul's is over there!
We can just see the dome peeping out from above the wall.
EXT. LONDON - BISHOPS LANE - DAY
MICAWBER and YOUNG DAVID reach the end of the alley, now back out onto the street, past MARKET STALLS.
Factory is that way, hundred yards, right, second left. Work hard! (running backwards now) Procrastination is the thief of time, my young friend - collar him!
MICAWBER grabs an onion from a stall, spins round the corner.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY
YOUNG DAVID looks into a bottle, at his reflection.
(mimics MICAWBER) In short, sir, something shall turn up.
A vague image of MICAWBER in the bottle, mouthing the words.
Cork and cork and cork again! ...and cork again!
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY

Some months have passed. YOUNG DAVID is looking ragged, in the scruffy outfit we later see in scenes 103, 190 & 214. YOUNG DAVID now more sure of himself: He fills two crates with bottles at the same time, one on either side of him. YOUNG DAVID eats a sandwich with one hand while pasting a label on a bottle with the other. YOUNG DAVID carries two or three crates so he can't see where he's going - MEALY calls instructions...
Forward, forward, right, stop... YOUNG DAVID climbs on the bench and leaps off, pulling the lever with all his weight. It works. Cork is in. Big reaction from the watching BOYS.
He's a corker of a corker!
INT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - EVENING

YOUNG DAVID opens the front door and runs into the house. The MICAWBERS upbeat and happy. The room full of furniture. Brand new, distinctive curtains and a BUST of MICAWBER on a plinth. A big, oval-framed photographic portrait of the family on the wall.
My friend, something has turned up!
Sherry? I've ordered a rosewood chiffonier for the parlour.
And we should calculate the cost of putting bow-windows to the house.
EXT. LONDON - MANORS STREET - DAY
MICAWBER and 14-YEAR-OLD DAVID (played by an S/A) being chased by a MARKET-STALL OWNER.
If these persons don't remain in their appointed premises, I'm not sure it's quite playing the game.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY
DAVID, now adult, sees a YOUNG GIRL struggling with a lever.
Jump! Imagine you're an acrobat.
The GIRL jumps from a bench on to the lever, corks the
Good. Five a minute.
DAVID spots a BOY failing to keep up the pace.
Keep at it Wilson, else I have to cork six to make up for your four.
INT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - HALLWAY / LIVING ROOM - DAY
DAVID comes in through the front door. He looks tired. House looking barren. Hard times. The MICAWBERS and DAVID eating a small chicken.
I pray your day was more remarkable than mine?
It certainly involved a remarkable number of bottles.
If Mr Micawber had but a shilling for each bottle corked in your warehouse today...
I should still face a disheartening debt.
There's a knock at the door.
Are we expecting visitors?
Suddenly louder banging on the door. MR MICAWBER

A BABY is in a cot in the hall. The cot starts to slowly move sideways. MR MICAWBER runs over - the hall carpet is being dragged under the rotting bottom of the front door, the cot riding on it. The BABY has a very large look of surprise on its face. The BABY tries to grab at the THIN PLINTH holding Micawber's BUST. MICAWBER goes to grab both the bust and the BABY: Does he go for baby or bust, baby or bust, baby or... the bust topples and falls as he grabs the BABY. It smashes. The door bursts open and BAILIFFS storm in.
We are undone! The sun goes down upon us! The debtors' prison awaits!
The BAILIFFS start to carry furniture out, loading it onto a hand cart, including the chairs they're sitting on. One CHILD pulls down one of the distinctive curtains and rolls it up under their arm.
EXT. MICAWBER'S HOUSE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
MR MICAWBER is manhandled out by TWO CONSTABLES. DAVID follows.
At least let him finish his meal you malicious apes!
Leave him be! Take your hands off that precious man!
A roast chicken is brought out. MR MICAWBER grabs a leg.
This is not your chicken! You are stealing an honest man's chicken!
Have a heart! Are your mothers proud of you?
EXT. STREET OUTSIDE MICAWBER'S HOUSE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
They spot another CHILD who's clambered onto a Bailiff's cart. They get him/her off in time. DAVID, at the last minute, spots his trunk being driven away on the cart, his jacket sticking out.
Stop! Forgive my earlier comments!
He runs and just manages to get his jacket with the St Paul's tin in the pocket. The cart disappears with everything else.
EXT. MARSHALSEA DEBTORS' PRISON - EVENING
Establisher of the awful prison. The MICAWBERS are being bundled out of the carriage.
This is a calumny! This isn't legal!
Hands off Micawber! He bruises like a peach!
INT. MARSHALSEA DEBTORS' PRISON - EVENING
DAVID with the MICAWBERS. End of a meal. Using the family portrait (now frayed) as a table. DAVID uncomfortable and keen to get out of the cell.
We've eaten off our own faces. It seems that should be some sort of profound metaphor.
DAVID hurriedly seizes the pause to take his leave.
I'll visit again tomorrow.
DAVID begins to head off.
Now the house is seized, where will you live?
DAVID's reaction. This hadn't occurred to him.
Oh. I hadn't thought...
Is your gruff auntie whats-her-name still alive?
Betsey. I don't know. I just recall my mother saying she lived at Dover and was...
(interrupting) My dear young friend! You have not been a lodger. To Mrs M and I, you've been a friend. Thank you.
He goes to leave, but MICAWBER keeps hold of his hand.
It behoves me to do something to help you out of your current difficulty.
MICAWBER scribbles and hands a note to DAVID with ceremony.
A Wilkins Micawber IOU. As good a promissory note as any issued from Threadneedle Street.
I honestly don't know how to thank you for this.
DAVID finally walks away.
Master David!
DAVID pauses yet again.
I only wish it could be more. Yes.
(gives a little wave) Until something turns up.
INT. MARSHALSEA DEBTORS' PRISON - HALLWAY - EVENING
Someone closes and locks the cell door behind DAVID, who rounds a corner and walks away. We hear concertina 'music' again. DAVID flinches.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - NIGHT

DAVID beds down among the bottles, using his St Paul's box as a pillow and his jacket as a blanket. Wide TOP SHOT of DAVID: Asleep, surrounded by bottles. He turns, and knocks a bottle which falls and smashes.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY
DAVID at work. Gazes up and see the sombre face of MURDSTONE.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - CREAKLE'S OFFICE - DAY
Both MURDSTONES stand grimly with CREAKLE and TUNGAY. Beside DAVID, MURDSTONE seems shorter now. He carries a walking cane.
Your stepfather informs meMe.
-that your mama is ill.
How ill is she? Tell him.
Jane!
MISS MURDSTONE
Tell me, please.
I won't deceive you. Very ill. Very ill. Very ill? Very ill?
She's dangerously ill. She's dead.
MURDSTONE and CREAKLE both turn admonishingly to TUNGAY, who realises he's made a mistake. DAVID tries not to cry.
(almost tearful) I'm very sorry.
And her funeral?
On Saturday. Saturday.
Saturday. Ought I to come back with you now, or take a later coach?
No, it was this Saturday just gone. She's buried.
We didn't want a fuss. Fuss.
A terrible beat. DAVID picks up a nearby empty bottle and approaches MURDSTONE, who cowers slightly.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - STAIRS - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
DAVID storms out, lets the bottle drop - SMASH - to the floor. A whoop from the other BOYS. CREAKLE and co rush out to stop him.
Copperfield! I will allow you that, you are upset, but do not... Do not..
CREAKLE exits the office with TUNGAY. David picks up another.
I've got no-one. I've got nothing! D'you hear me? Nothing! Smash.
Right, that's half a day's pay. ...pay.
Half of nothing is nothing.
DAVID smashes another bottle after pouring the oily contents all over the floor. MURDSTONE and MISS MURDSTONE emerge.
Given the manner of your overreaction, it's a good thing you were not at the funeral. And another bottle - smash!
You can't take something from some one who has nothing. Nothing.
Think about your future.
Apart from your aunt Betsey you are without blood relatives.
DAVID smashes another bottle.
I've got you, and you are nothing!
DAVID sweeps a whole shelf of bottles onto the floor with an almighty SMASH! The other BOYS yell with delight. It's chaos. DAVID runs out the building, calling out..
I want more! I want more than this! Far more! I deserve something! I will be something! Something.
This is nothing! (to the MURDSTONES, who cower) You two are ghosts. You've always been dead!
MURDSTONE makes the same undignified noise he made earlier with Young David, as DAVID leaves, taking his St Paul's box. Quiet! QUIET!
EXT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY - LATE AFTERNOON
DAVID rushes out of the warehouse, pushing past some BOYS.
EXT. LONDON STREET - DAY - LATE AFTERNOON
DAVID walks down a narrow London street.
EXT. LONDON - CROWDED WESTMINSTER BRIDGE - DAY

WIDE as DAVID walks across a busy bridge. No pedestrians, but he threads through horses and carriages in a traffic jam. The PALACE of WESTMINSTER and ELIZABETH TOWER being constructed in the background.
EXT. LONDON SUBURB - DAY
DAVID walking through parkland. On the horizon in the distance is London, that DAVID has left behind.
EXT. TRACK OUTSIDE FRONT OF HOUSE - DAY
A long well-maintained path stretches into a forest. DAVID large in the foreground, walking away from us.
EXT. DRIVEWAY WITH DOVER SIGN - DAY

Sign in foreground. Dover, 23 Miles. The road now poorly maintained. We can just see the small figure of DAVID, walking with his jacket over his shoulder. A quick altercation with a MAN in his 30s who is coming the other way. He runs off, towards us, with DAVID'S jacket.
EXT. DOVER DOWNS - NEXT DAY
The bare, wide downs. Vast image of sea and sky. DAVID, a small dot, no shoes, stops, stares to sea. END OF MONTAGE.
EXT. A DOVER STREET - DAY

DAVID, now very dirty, with a STREET SWEEPER, who points. STREET SWEEPER Used to be Mrs Collins, but she's Miss Trotwood again now. Good luck, she's as fierce as a birthing badger. DAVID heads off, apprehensive.
EXT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - DAY

An exhausted DAVID approaches a house on a green. The sea in the distance. He's staggering slightly, his feet bleeding, but still carrying the St Paul's box. A WOMAN RIDER and a CHILD ride donkeys on the green. BETSEY is outside, gardening. Then...
Janet! Donkeys! DONKEYS!
She runs inside as a housemaid, JANET, runs out banging a pan with a ladle.
Go on! Go away!
Then BETSEY reappears, smashing a dinner gong.
Shooh! Off my green or I'll box your ears!
The donkey refuses to move, so BETSEY kicks its front leg. It buckles and the WOMAN RIDER slides off onto BETSEY. A man appears at an upper window - MR DICK.
(calls to BETSEY) Somebody! Quick question. King Charles the First - we're certain that he's dead?
When last seen in public his head was no longer attached to his body.
Good. Thank you. Much obliged.
He disappears. JANET leads the donkeys away. BETSEY dumps her gong on the floor and gets back to gardening.
(to DAVID, not looking at him) No young men needed here. Shoo shoo! I've got a garden fork!
I am not just a young man, ma'am...
I'll summon a constable.
DAVID snaps, grabs the pan and ladle. Clangs loudly.
No. Listen! Listen! You're my aunt! Janet!
I'm your nephew! I'm David Copperfield...
BETSEY drops to the ground in astonishment.
Sorry. (begins helping her up) I'm David Copperfield. From the Rookery. JANET!
I've been ill-used and put to work not fit for me and you're the only family I have... Mr DICK!
She leads DAVID towards the house.
I have walked all the way here from London, and I was robbed, and I've barely eaten, and haven't slept in a bed since I set out...
Come inside. Do not touch anything. Mr Dick! Janet! Mr Dick!
INT/EXT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - PARLOUR - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
BETSEY leads DAVID in through the French doors. The house is bright, fresh.
Stand there, yes.
(staggering) I'm sorry... Everything is
circular... I'm going to drop...
He's about to collapse! Janet! Mind the settee, it's Viennese! No there, there, go there!

JANET throws a rug on the sofa where DAVID looks like he'll fall, but, now unconscious, he falls into a very smart armchair, to BETSEY's distress. DAVID briefly fainted. From DAVID'S POV: BETSEY now has some bottles and pours the contents into DAVID's mouth.
What are you doing?
Medicine. Reviving you.
This is salad dressing.
Ah. I thought it was Armagnac. No spectacles on.
Do you have a lettuce somewhere, doused in medicine?
MR DICK shouts from the stairs.
Head entirely removed? We're sure?
He appears at the door.
(signalling - 'Not now') Let's leave Charles's head to one side for now.
Pick it up later. Understood. (to DAVID, smiling) How do you do?
BETSEY and MR DICK begin talking over DAVID's head.
(signals for MR DICK to look at her) Now, Mr Dick, don't be a fool because nobody can be more discerning than you, when you choose.
MR DICK is immediately serious and solemn.
(a lacy shawl round DAVID) You have heard me mention my brother David Copperfield?
BETSEY takes DICK aside. JANET back in now. She picks up a
(doesn't remember at all) Yes, just now. Oh, you mean you mentioned it before this moment? Of course you did, I remember it well. I'm hungry.
(focusing on cakes) Cakes. Those are cakes.
JANET beside DAVID with the plate, at his head height. Grabs a cake and gives it to MR DICK. DAVID looks on eagerly.
Well, this is his son, who's run away. What shall we do with him?
DAVID looks hungrily at the plate of cakes. Almost takes one.
One thing you could do is...
If I were you - I should wash him!
(relieved to have an answer) Janet! Heat the bath! (she's right behind her) Oh, you're there.
DAVID about to take a cake as JANET moves off. She leaves the cakes on a small table.
The thing is, I haven't eaten since...
(to DAVID) Mr Dick cracks it every time! (glancing out the window, hands DICK the salad dressing) More donkeys! Good Lord, there must be fifty. Janet!!

DICK takes a seat, inspecting the salad dressing, as BETSEY runs out with JANET through a side door. Through the French doors we see, played out in silence, BETSEY arrive with a broom and swing it at the riders. DAVID and DICK, each eating a big slice of cake, watch. A TALL TEENAGE BOY leads a donkey.
Is my aunt really going to...
Visit violence upon the boy? Yes.
(faint, through the window) I've warned you. Don't say you weren't warned...

From their POV we see BETSEY grab the TALL TEENAGE BOY with one hand, and slam his head against a signpost which reads "NO DONKEYS!" He runs off, she turns, strolls back towards the house. MR DICK laughs.
Remarkable woman. Very kind.
He turns to DAVID, but he's now sound asleep on the sofa.
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - GUEST BEDROOM - DAY
Morning. DAVID dressing in Mr Dick's clothes, in front of a long mirror. Tentatively, he acts out some characters.
(as MR DICK) "Head taken off? We're sure?" (as BETSEY) "Donkeys. I've got a fork!" (as Tungey) "Donkeys..got a fork "
Delighted, he gets confident. The mirror seems to contain the actual character, not DAVID. The face of MICAWBER appears.
You're stealing an honest man's chicken!
His sole possession - the St Paul's box - sits on the window
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - LANDING - DAY (CONTINUOUS) 85
Through a doorway, MR DICK - dressed almost identically - is writing with a long pen, his head almost laid upon the paper.
Good morning, Mr Dick.
Ah, young man. Can you form a queue?
David unsure of how to do this on his own.
The capital letter Q. I'm trying different forms.
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - MR DICK'S ROOM - DAY

A cluttered room. Piles of paper, pens, inkpots, pen-knives, paperweights. The bed at a mad angle. A small model of the room made from scavenged items, or straw and string. Donkeys made of straw. One side (with the models) is quite neat, the other a chaos of papers. A big kite prominent in the corner. MR DICK writes, trying different formations of a capital 'Q'. I like this: a cigar in an ashtray.
Or a kite with a string. MR DICK (distracted)
"The executioner's blade is cold
I'm sorry...?
(normal, points at kite) Your aunt me that kite, to get me out the house while she drinks
coffee and is quiet.
Are you writing stories, Mr Dick? About King Charles the First? MR DICK (alarmed, amazed) Why? What makes you say that?
(gesturing to papers) There seems to be the occasional reference to him on...

We see MR DICK's papers are full of drawings of Charles, his signature 'CHARLES R', chopped heads, complicated doodles. There are piles of these scraps of paper, looming over MR DICK, close to falling over.

(reassured) Oh yes. King Charles. He does creep in. You see, I'm trying to draft a petition calling for improved housing conditions for the labouring poor. I work hard at it but the thoughts in King Charles' head keep intruding.
I understand. (a beat) Sorry, no I don't. King Charles's head?
MR DICK nods. Points to his own nodding head.
I believe, owing to a disagreement, they cut off Charles's head.
In 1649. That's well documented.
Well, for some reason I don't fully understand, they removed all the

and put them, instead, into mine. (becoming more agitated) Look at this... (shows DAVID) ...I write them down! It's most disruptive. (to himself) "I mount the scaffold, wearing two shirts so shivers aren't mistaken for fear."
MR DICK looks away, upset, then into the mirror. Seems to correct himself. DAVID sees, on a table, a vase of flowers with the heads cut off and arranged around the bottom.
I know it must all sound peculiar?
By no means. Something similar happens to me.
(excited / suspicious) Really? Who do you get? Not Charles?
No, no. But I find when I've been in the company of some person of strong character, their voice becomes... lodged in my head. I often wonder whether I'm...
Different in some way.
Out of your mind.
DAVID jumps up, runs out the room. A beat, then he's back with his St Paul's box. Opens it. Full of scraps of paper.
I too write down the thoughts I have, and the things I observe.
MR DICK reads some of DAVID's writing.
"Miss Murdstone's sleepless eyes, like two red suns." Very good. "The bottles are propelled by iron pistons that nod up and down like melancholy mad elephants."
What d'you think?
Oh, just right. And excellent calligraphy. Your 'L' here looks
like the handle of a butter-churn and the 'K' like a folding chair. The dinner gong is banged downstairs.
Breakfast! Breakfast!
That'll be breakfast. (he starts to turn away from DAVID, troubled) "At my final breakfast I hear the mob gather to witness my death..."
(excited) We'll banish that mob, Mr Dick!
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - PARLOUR - DAY
BETSEY at breakfast, eating boiled eggs. Severing the top of one. DAVID runs down.
Aunt - Mr Dick. Is he at all...?
Did he mention Charles the First?
A little. Actually, quite a lot.
That's his allegorical way of expressing it. He connects his illness with great disturbance and agitation. But his mind is sharp as a surgeon's lancet, make no mistake.
DAVID has a sudden thought.
I think I may be able to help him.
Then go back up, Trotwood. Janet can soft-boil an egg in a flash.
DAVID goes to exit, comes back.
...um...sorry, Trotwood?
I've been thinking that I might call you Trotwood. If I'm to financially support my nephew I want to like his name.
INT. BESTEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - MR DICK'S ROOM - DAY
DAVID comes back in. MR DICK is writing like a demon, piling up scraps of paper. He's upset and agitated.
(excited) Mr Dick! The troubling thoughts from King Charles's head - they weigh you down?

(looking at the pile) (to himself) "As I die I go from a corruptible to an incorruptible crown." (holds up his pen) I throw them in there(nods to overflowing wastepaper basket) But they pile up and oppress me.
(trying to copy Betsey's gesture to snap Mr Dick out of it) But we can release them, Mr Dick. We can cast them to the wind.
David looks at the kite. MR DICK follows his gaze.
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - PARLOUR - DAY (CONTINUOUS) 89
BETSEY is reading while having coffee. Wondering what the commotion is upstairs, but smiling. Kite time!
MR DICK (O.S.)
MR DICK and DAVID power through the room and out into the garden, brushing past a large vase of flowers / greenery on the way.
EXT. BETSEY'S HOUSE - DAY - MOMENTS LATER
DAVID is with MR DICK. The kite is now plastered with MR DICK's writing and drawings. Dense text all over it. We go up in the air with it. The sky is absorbing the troubling
The higher the words go...
The more clear my mind becomes. Oh, the clarity!
MR DICK changes, seem to become less tense, less twitchy, warmer, more naturally conversational.

It's like I'm reading for the bar again, just before it all... and I shared a staircase with Tommy Traddles, who had a comic head of hair and was such a terrific fellow - "un camarade tres formidable," as the French say. There's a thing I'd completely forgotten I can speak French! This is a remarkable day.
It's a delight to see you so liberated.
What will you do?
About what?
Your thoughts? What will you do with them?
Oh, I like my thoughts.
But the voices. They are real?
Yes. It's as if my head were a room with many visitors. They come and go, like...a breath.
We're going to be the best of friends. (looking up, the kite is falling) Oh dear. Oh dear. (to himself, reverting to old Mr Dick) "I can hear them build the scaffold
and hone the axe's blade..."
Let's run faster to keep it in the air. Look, it's rising.
They run faster.
Up it goes again. Three hundred hurrahs plus half a dozen imperial woo-hoos! My mind is clear...
...As a soap-bubble!
They continue to run in the summer sunshine.
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - PARLOUR - DAY (CONTINUOUS) 91
They come back in past BETSEY, as she and JANET are putting the greenery/flowers back into a fresh arrangement.
This boy is terrific. Thank you, erm...
Trotwood. David's son. MR DICK Thank you, Trotwood Davidson.
A pleasure, sir. Lovely flowers.
EXT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - DAY
A set of WIND CHIMES tinkle in the breeze. Kite time!
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - STAIRWELL - DAY

Passage of time. We watch a military manoeuvre from BETSEY and JANET. The table moved to one side, a HUGE VASE full of ferns held, standard lamp secured. Then MR DICK comes haring down the stairs with his kite, covered in writing and drawings of Charles 1st, and out through to the parlour. DAVID follows.
EXT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - DAY (MID SUMMER)
Passage of time. Outside BETSEY's. A SMALL BOY on a donkey is being led over the green by a BOY. Donkeys!
DAVID comes out, clanging the pan with the ladle. A wild gesture to scare them off. Slightly undignified.
Shoo! I'll tan your hide and put you in a stew! I'm a huge maniac!
He clangs, not noticing a white-haired man and his daughter MR WICKFIELD and AGNES have appeared. DAVID pauses clanging, turns, sees them, is deeply embarrassed. Drops the ladle. A beat.
We suffer from a plague of donkeys. You may think I am exaggerating, which perhaps I am.
AGNES smiles.
Very ferocious shoo-ing. You must be Trotwood. I'm Wickfield. I act for your aunt in matters of finance. My daughter Agnes...
DAVID goes for a handshake with WICKFIELD, but turns it into a bow to AGNES, quite low.
A bow! I am so rarely bowed to.
I hope I've started a new fashion. Unless you deem it inappropriate?
Not at all Trotwood! I shall demand it at our every meeting from now on, as if I am an Empress. Or mad.
BETSEY comes out.
Ah, Mr Wickfield! Please, come in...
That was quite the journey. Is it too early for sherry?
A little early.
Port then? It's seven in the evening in Singapore. I imagine.
But scarcely dawn in Newfoundland...
A look between AGNES and BETSEY, which DAVID clocks.
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - PARLOUR - MOMENTS LATER

AGNES and DAVID are sat close to the hallway door. BETSEY rushes past WICKFIELD, picks up a bottle of sherry and hands it to JANET without WICKFIELD seeing. JANET subtly sticks it in a drinks cabinet on wheels, shaped like an antique globe. Closes it. WICKFIELD stands, restless.
(sitting down) Mr Wickfield owns the freehold of a very good educational establishment in Canterbury.
She nods to JANET to start wheeling the Globe off.
It's snapping at the heels of the better known establishments.
I have a thirst for education that sadly has never been quenched.
Really? You give the impression of having a very well watered intellect.
There's a CLINK from the Globe. AGNES subtly gestures to JANET to stop.
All this talk of thirst is making me thirsty...
The clinking attracts WICKFIELD's attention. BETSEY stands up, to block him.
How is business, Mr Wickfield?
WICKFIELD skirts around her. JANET is forced to move aside.
(too quick) All is well. (a beat of reflection) There are, of course, challenges reduced tariffs, the retreat from mercantilism. (MORE)
(he's by the globe now) Here and in Europe, the Americas... (pokes the globe, hoping to open it) ...Africa. I could go on. (he does) India...
'All is well' would have sufficed.
MR DICK appears at the door.
(to AGNES) Good afternoon, I'm Mr Dick. I am very partial to gingerbread.
As am I Mr Dick. I adore the fiery taste. Delighted to meet you.
(to DAVID, pleased) "Delighted." (to the room) Could you confirm something for me, if you wouldn't mind?
DAVID, JANET and BETSEY do the 'not now' gesture to MR DICK. He doesn't notice.
Yes?
It remains- does it not? - attached to my body?
(totally unfazed) I'm looking at you now Mr Dick and I can confirm without any doubt that they are.
Good to hear. Would you like to see an amazing kite?
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - MR DICK'S ROOM - DAY

The chaos of MR DICK's room. DAVID and AGNES excited and busy on the floor, cutting up bits of manuscript, handing them to MR DICK. DICK pasting by dipping paper into a SAUCER full of glue, and turning to slap it onto the kite. Occasionally pausing to write down a thought.
He believes that when Charles the First was executed, the King's troubles flew from his head to nest in Mr Dick's own.
Is that why you fly them on your kite, Mr Dick, to rid yourself of them? Precisely.
Well, it is the obvious course of action.
Trotwood suggested it. He is a marvel.
Watches MR DICK swivel from notes to kite.
Mr Dick, you look like you're playing the kettle-drums.
DAVID laughs.
He does, of course! (to AGNES) You should write that down.
Yes, ready for the next time I see
someone pasting things at speed to
Oh - I like you.
What a happy coincidence, because I like you too!
INT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - PARLOUR - DAY
BETSEY and MR WICKFIELD now sitting down. The Globe has been moved over to the French doors.
(staring at the Globe) I will arrange for Trotwood to board with Mrs Strong. This possibly calls for a celebration...
(claps) Hooray!
I was thinking more along the lines of...
Tea? Janet!
(resigned) Tea would be fine. Kite time!
MR DICK (O.S.)
JANET rushes out. A moment later, MR DICK leads AGNES and DAVID through. DICK steps back in for a moment, and opens the Globe.
Why not have a big glass of port wine, Mr Wickfield, you do love it so.
I do, Mr Dick. I'm touched that you remember.
EXT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - GREEN - DAY (END OF SUMMER) 98
A picnic, which includes a bowl of soft-boiled eggs. BETSEY, MR DICK (his kite flying), DAVID and AGNES. BETSEY has a letter of admission from the school (or a Good Luck card), signed by Wickfield.
I shall miss our picnics when you go away to board, and a certain monarch starts to creep into my head.
Trot will visit, and we can visit him...
(cuts in) I meant Charles The First. Yes.
(ignoring) ..and there will be plenty more picnics. And dinners, and teas, with...
Buttered toast, piled high...in...haystacks. MR DICK Haystacks! Very good.
So huge we'll serve them with pitchforks!
And coffee, tar-black and hissing hot.
This is now a game. BETSEY enjoying it.
Hissing hot. Excellent. Good words. Agnes?
And fine dinners. Pot-bellied baskets of blackened chestnuts and long wreaths of sausages...
DAVID's turn - a new sense of focus.
(cuts in) Bottles of straw-coloured drinks, ripened long ago in lands where no fogs are, sparkling after their nap
and pushing at their corks to help the corkscrew, like prisoners
Everyone goes quiet for a beat: he's won.
Your mind operates at a rolling boil, Trot. You'll enjoy Mrs Strong's establishment - it's not in an ideal condition, but means well.
(a joke, but meant) A little like my father.
MR DICK laughs. BETSEY frowns.
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - FRONT - DAY
A carriage pulls up. DAVID helps AGNES out. Goes to pick up his trunk but URIAH HEEP rushes over to take it.
Don't trouble yourself...
It's not even a bit of trouble to help. It's pure Christian pleasure.
MRS STRONG, principal of the establishment, arrives to see MR WICKFIELD unsteadily and inelegantly descend backwards. URIAH helps him, getting very close to AGNES.
This way sir...
Steady there, steady! These steps are lethal. Very, very high...
Uriah, please, there's no need...
I can't do it. I'll get back in.
You're getting close. Come on - one foot...then...that's it.
WICKFIELD lands awkwardly, turns to MRS STRONG.
Mrs Strong. Welcome. No, sorry, I'm welcome aren't I? You're already here. Sorry, my head is muddled...
From the bumpy journey.
Very bumpy, seemingly.
This is Cropwood Trotterfield.
(shakes her hand) Pleased to meet you, Mrs Strong. What do you have in your hand?
(quietly, to DAVID) It's nothing.
(upbeat) It's a very small piece of wall. But all is well. Come this way.
As she turns away, the smile leaves MRS STRONG's face.
INT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - CORRIDOR - DAY (CONTINUOUS) MRS STRONG and DAVID walk through a dilapidated wreck of a
building. URIAH trails behind them. Several holes in the wall. MRS STRONG straightens a portrait to go over one, moves a bench to hide another. Fits the bit of plaster she's carrying into a third.
The place may need a little
decoration, once Mr Wickfield's funds are more fluid.
It's very...
He looks up. A BOY peers down through a hole in the ceiling. ...airy.
DAVID (CONT'D)
MRS STRONG opens a door by booting the bottom with force. She then swaps a full bucket catching a drip with a BOY, who swaps it for an empty one. She catches DAVID's reaction to this routine.
Like many of the great old
INT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - CLASSROOM - DAY

The class, in long pews facing inwards over a table, chat. Most eyes on an older boy - STEERFORTH - smart, expensively styled hair. He has a cane which is slightly too short. He talks to another boy, MARKHAM, and the class in general.
Happens on this day once a month. Twelve o'clock on the dot, Mr Sharp
pretends to visit the barber... MRS STRONG and DAVID enter.
...and then comes back an hour later wearing the shortest of his three wigs!
(to DAVID) We'll wait until Mr Steerforth finishes his funny story about the geography master.
MARKHAM and the other BOYS laugh at STEERFORTH's story. STEERFORTH sees MRS STRONG, stands. On his cue, so do the others. DAVID suddenly feels all eyes are on him.
MRS STRONG (CONT'D)
This is Copperfield. He's new. STEERFORTH looks DAVID up and down, inspecting him.
I'd surmised as much, Mrs Strong, but appreciate the confirmation.
Haha. Very good Mr Steerforth.
(continues to study DAVID) Good buttons on that jacket...
(to DAVID) As you see, we currently aren't at capacity. A variety of things keeping students away - holidays, family events, influenza...
But certainly not a better education elsewhere, eh Mrs Strong?
The class laugh. URIAH arrives with DAVID's bags.
Very good. Again. Hahahaha.
(slightly mocking of MRS STRONG's laugh) Hahahaha. Haha!
MARKHAM
URIAH has pushed through to DAVID, shakes his hand.
Thrilled to make your acquaintance, Master Copperfield.
MRS STRONG exits.
(exiting, to URIAH) Bring those to the dorm please.
(bowing low to DAVID) I am in deep humility.
He exits, leaving the door open. We occasionally see him pass with bits of luggage.
And with that, Uriah Heep rubbed himself out of the room. (offers his hand)
(nervous) Davidson...no, David Copper...no, sorry, Trotwood. Copperfield.
Is that all hyphenated?
Laughs from the class. DAVID unsure of how to respond.
You see, my aunt calls me...
(interrupting) And what do you make of our friend Heep?
He's perplexing.
Perplexing.
An interesting word. Perplexing how?
It's difficult to describe.
Half a beat, eyes on DAVID, who is unsure of his ground.
He twitches his mouth like a curious lizard.
(big laugh) Ha! He does. Tell me another thing.
He stands so close by that he's
nearer to you than your own shirt.
Perfect! You're sharp as a whip, I like you. Sit here. Markham, shift over, don't be a lump.
(to MARKHAM) Sorry.
MARKHAM, peeved, shuffles down the bench. The BOY on the end has to get up and go to the other side. DAVID now beside STEERFORTH. A notch more relaxed.
Heep was once a pupil here. Charity case. One feels sorry for boys of such background of course...
(too quick) Indeed. I do. Very sorry.
A bit of plaster drops from the ceiling. Heads!
Forgive the collapsing. Old Wickfield's funds are drying up.
Unlike the man himself.
Loves his drink. Do you know Wickfield?
(impersonating WICKFIELD) "Is it too early for a sherry?"
DAVID mimes Wickfield drinking. STEERFORTH laughs. DAVID's tension eases further.
Here's Wickfield threading a needle!
STEERFORTH mimes Wickfield's hand-shaking. Cruel, but funny. URIAH peers round the door. DAVID half-stands.
Master Copperfield, I have left your bags next to...
Boring. Not interested. Off you creep, Heep!
The class laugh. Including, guiltily, DAVID. URIAH is humiliated but angry as hell. Slinks out as MRS STRONG reenters. DAVID stands; he's the only one.
Isn't Mr Mell supposed to be taking
(pulling DAVID down) He's not here, Mrs Strong.
Oh no - not...?
Yes. Last seen halfway to Broadstairs with a barmaid.
MRS STRONG closes her eyes briefly. Then re-composes herself.
INT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - DORMITORY - NIGHT

DAVID with STEERFORTH, unpacking. The St Paul's box is in DAVID's new trunk, with the 'HE BITES' placard and a lot of IOUs from Micawber. MARKHAM speaks to another couple of BOYS in the background. As DAVID sits on his bed, it wobbles. STEERFORTH grabs two bricks, smashes one with the other, props it up.
I'm glad you've arrived. I'd become so bored of the same tedious fools spouting their predictable noise.
STEERFORTH and DAVID realise MARKHAM has heard this. A beat.
But no offence meant, Markham.
Big laugh from STEERFORTH. MARKHAM feigns a laugh.
Nice cut to that waistcoat - who's your tailor?
Just - a man in Dover. My aunt took me...
So your aunt brought you up?
Well, she...um... (wants to end this...) ...she's certainly brought me up sharp a few times! She's a tartar!
Ha! The very daisy of the field is not fresher than you are. I shall call you Daisy - will you mind?
(Yes) Not at all.
Why on earth would you...'Daisy'?
I have currant wine here and some almond cakes, if you'd like?

DAVID goes over to STEERFORTH's area. It's like a private room, made of scavenged items: a pile of big old books as a wash-stand; a dead grandfather clock, the inside of which is full of waistcoats and shirts, the clock face removed, stood on bricks to act as a bedside table. And where the clock face was, a shaving kit. A smart jacket hangs on a tailor's dummy. STEERFORTH pours some wine.
You haven't got a sister, have you Daisy? No.
Oh. That's a pity.
Hands the glass to DAVID.
INT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - DORM - NIGHT
The dorm is quiet. Most boys asleep, including MARKHAM. Embers glow in the fireplace. DAVID reading by candlelight, his bed near STEERFORTH's.
(restless) What are you reading?
Peregrine Pickle. I found it in the library here.
We have a library?
Well, the stack of books propping up the cricket scoreboard.
Ah. Then read to me. I can't sleep.
DAVID suddenly nervous. The book's words start to wobble and slide as they did with Murdstone.
This book's quite long. Why don't I tell you a story of mine? Perhaps one about a kindly nurse and her charge...
PEGGOTTY and YOUNG DAVID appear.
...who slept in an upturned boat...
No. I don't care for whimsy. Sorry.
PEGGOTTY and YOUNG DAVID slope off.
Do you have a scary story?
I have one about...an evil schoolmaster?
Yes! What does he look like?
A hideous one-eyed version of MURDSTONE emerges from STEERFORTH's tailor's dummy, wearing a similar jacket.
He has but one eye when the popular prejudice runs in favour of two...
MURDSTONE has an eye patch. STEERFORTH laughs.
His hair... (he thinks) ...he has none at all.
MURDSTONE now completely bald. This is DAVID controlling him.
What's the man's name?
It's Murd-i-stone! Oh, he is cruel. He viciously beats any boy who doesn't know his lesson.
The monster! Is there vengeance? Is he himself thrashed and battered?
He has an equally cruel sister.
Ah, you see, he's got a sister!
She hangs a hard steel bag on her
arm by a heavy chain, and is a cold
A metal pillar or a water pipe with valve becomes JANE MURDSTONE holding her metallic bag. She looks a complete mess.
She takes no care of her appearance or hygiene and she punches her brother, to goad him on to further savagery!
JANE MURDSTONE punches the bald MURDSTONE in the side of his head. He whimpers.
MISS MURDSTONE
Jane!
STEERFORTH hears something outside.
Heep's up. Into bed.

DAVID jumps back into bed just before URIAH HEEP silently opens the door and looks in. The MURDSTONES have gone. URIAH creeps around, counts the boys, and then begins opening the odd drawer and trunk, peering in.
Good. Fine. All fine.
Then he exits. A beat. MR MICAWBER appears in the shadows.
Shall I tell you the tale of the insolvent but ever-hopeful...
No. No more stories Daisy. Sorry, but I need to sleep.
STEERFORTH turns over in bed as MICAWBER scratches his head and wanders off.
INT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY

DAVID and STEERFORTH stand in the factory, watching the earlier scene of bottle-smashing, but fictionalised and more stylized now by DAVID. Thick shards of light break across the room. MURDSTONE, JANE MURDSTONE, CREAKLE and TUNGAY are all present, but it's YOUNG DAVID who holds a bottle.
I shall smash it!
Then I shall smash you! Jane! Jane!
(quietly) Be quiet. QUIET!
Tungay! Me!
(quietly) Be quiet. QUIET!
YOUNG DAVID smashes the bottle.
MISS MURDSTONE

MURDSTONE approaches YOUNG DAVID. YOUNG DAVID punches MURDSTONE in the jaw and he falls into a cart full of bottles, taking JANE with him. They both scream. The cart starts to move towards the huge rickety-looking structure of bottles. Instead of crashing into the structure the cart curves away, tips over and bottles fall in two directions. One bottle hits a strut, which props up the huge structure. The strut falls, and the huge structure collapses immediately. A terrible, hilarious CRASH.
And you make these tales of the factory boy up out of thin air?
Invented, yes.
The MURDSTONES and a horrified TUNGAY and CREAKLE watch, as YOUNG DAVID escapes.
EXT. CANTERBURY STREETS - DAY
DAVID and STEERFORTH walk through shelves of bottles and step out into the middle of the street. Rows of bottles can be seen in the foreground, on a MARKET STALL.
I could see the boy like he was actually there, Daisy. You truly are the Eighth Wonder.
You seem to know all the details about the factory - was your father in manufacturing?
My stepfather...
(suspects something?) You have a stepfather but were brought up by an aunt...?
(changes subject, pointing) Who is that, Steerforth?
A young lady, MISS LARKINS, is climbing into a carriage.
Ah - that's the eldest Miss Larkins. Pretty, isn't she?
She is a blaze of beauty.
She's engaged to an army captain.
Only because she has yet to meet me...
They laugh, then...
Look out! Couple of Mrs Strong's prize poodles have got loose!
Across the road, behind a MEAT STALL is a BUTCHER'S BOY, DAVID's age, hair greased flat. Looks like a boxer.
Oi! Ladies! Come here! I'll beat you with one hand tied behind me.
DAVID is aware that MISS LARKINS is clocking this.
(shouts to BUTCHER'S BOY) You want to fight do you? Then sir name your time!
Don't fight him. Promise me you won't fight him.
EXT. BUTCHER'S SHOP YARD - DAY
DAVID ready to fight. Surrounded by meat detritus, off-cuts, bits of hooves, blood in metal buckets, and PEOPLE.
You can box, I take it Daisy?
After a fashion, certainly...
Gentlemen! No eyeball-gouging, no...actually everything else is allowed. Get set...fight!!
The crowd roars. DAVID starts dancing around, loosening up, raises his fists to...BANG!! BUTCHER'S BOY has run and smashed a fist into his face. DAVID flailing.
Hook, feint, uppercut! DAVID turns, hazy and staggering, towards STEERFORTH.

Wallop! The BUTCHER'S BOY slams in for a sudden, crunching punch that knocks DAVID out. Brief blackness. DAVID comes to. He's fallen into a pile of straw and meat off-cuts. He's just missed a SHEEP'S HEAD. STEERFORTH, anxious to avoid the filth, helps him up by offering him his cane. Pulling himself up, DAVID nearly knocks over a sloshing bucket of blood.
You did, in some ways, very well Daisy. But a gentleman shouldn't be beaten by a Butcher's Boy. Presumably we must buy steak for your eye from the self-same fellow.
DAVID, covered in mud and some animal blood, looks through the arch into the street. MISS LARKINS is walking by with a FRIEND. She pauses, looks at him, horrified.
Let's get you to Wickfield's house.
EXT. WICKFIELD'S HOUSE - DAY
Establisher of Mr Wickfield's townhouse. STEERFORTH brings DAVID inside.
Come on. I'm afraid you've been butchered, dear Daisy.
INT. WICKFIELD'S HOUSE - SITTING ROOM - DAY
A SMALL BOY with a bandaged head walks past STEERFORTH. DAVID on a sofa. URIAH, perched on the arm, tends to DAVID's black eye with a piece of steak, his cut lip with hot water and iodine.
There's a degree of animal blood, from the meat(to STEERFORTH) -as well as Master Copperfield's own essence.
Try not to die or anything boring like that Daisy.
