I'll try my beURIAH places a big piece of cotton on DAVID's lip. STEERFORTH exits.
What a confident gentleman he is. (dabbing the cut lip) Mother has taught me the medical rudiments. She's the laundress here. Washes your bedsheets.
Always very clean. Relatively.
Oh! She will burst with gratitude that you've acknowledged her spontaneously, Master Copperfield. (getting closer to DAVID) Miss Wickfield, she's - she's very...do you not think?
(taking the meat off) Very...? What? Tall? Pleasant? Good at backgammon?
URIAH gives DAVID a look. Then AGNES enters. URIAH exits.
My dear Trotwood, they told me you were here. Oh dear, look at you. (then) But I imagine the other fellow must be dreadfully injured.
Oh yes - close to death. Measured for his coffin.
She sits beside DAVID on the sofa and begins tending to his injuries.
How were Uriah's ministrations?
He's like a human cold in the head!
He gets so close...
Yes! It's as if he lives up your
nose and is keen to get home. They laugh.
Did he mention his mother?
His mother?! Oh, Agnes! I burst with gratitude that you should ask me such a question spontaneously!
They laugh harder.
So is this Steerforth's doing, getting you into fights?
No! He tried to stop me. Doesn't think a gentleman should be seen fighting a butcher's boy.
A gentleman!
Yes. Steerforth thinks of me as a gentleman.
You are. (pause) So I'm assuming you haven't told him...?
No. I fear he might...This is the first time someone like that has regarded me as an equal. Except you, Agnes. And I think of you as... Special?
A sister.
A special sister.
Micro reaction from AGNES. Suddenly, URIAH has appeared between them, behind the sofa. AGNES gets up, putting space between herself and URIAH.
Might I be bold enough to ask you to come to tea? With me and mother.
What a shame! I fear I have a prior engagement on that date.
On which date? I don't believe I mentioned a date.
(holding in laughter) I don't believe you did.
I understand. It's not my place to invite the likes of you to tea.
DAVID stands.
No, no! I would...I would be glad to come, Mr Heep.
Uriah, please. Oh, Mother will go off like a rocket! Like a rocket! On the 14th, perhaps, at four? (laying a hand on AGNES' shoulder) And if Miss Wickfield would care...?
Sadly... (teasing DAVID) On the 14th at four...I have a prior engagement.
Of course! I am meant to join you in that engagement, am I not Agnes?
I don't believe so, no.
Good, then I can definitely come to tea...Uriah!
Oh! I am so proud to be noticed by you! I'm in ecstasy!
URIAH does another very low bow.
I will arrange things with mother.
AGNES visibly relaxes as URIAH heads for the door. She begins to move back towards the sofa.
(re steak) Are you done with that? It's a tolerably nice bit of rump. (to AGNES) No offence meant, I'm sure.
DAVID nods. URIAH takes the steak, bows low, exits.
INT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - CLASSROOM - DAY
DAVID now much more confident. Lounging around with STEERFORTH on a bench. All the BOYS chatting, relaxed.
Tell me more about the impecunious debtor hiding behind carcasses. (impression of DAVID) "There's something on the way!"
(as MICAWBER) "Something will turn up!" That's it!
As if by magic, MICAWBER's voice is heard.
An excellent precept, young man!

A beaming MICAWBER comes through the door. He's wearing a second-hand suit too big for him, a waistcoat made of the distinctive curtains, and a pair of pince-nez. YOUNG DAVID walks to his side, smiling. A story is coming alive again. Then MRS STRONG and URIAH HEEP come in behind him.
We have a new master joining us: Professor Micawber, MA Cantab! YOUNG DAVID disappears. Nod from MICAWBER to an amazed DAVID, who has his head down, avoiding eye contact.
(fiddles with glasses) Good morning boys. Be seated.
(whispering to David) Oh dear Lord, this place really must be short of money.
You lived with the Professor when you were in London I believe, Mr Copperfield?
The Professor, yes. Briefly, and at the same time lengthily.
Very nice! Very genteel.
URIAH exits. Followed by MRS STRONG.
Now! Which particular dish from the great feast of knowledge will it be our mutual privilege to partake in at this current juncture?
The boys look at him blankly.
The BLACKBOARD behind him contains mathematical sums and equations (but there's enough room for MICAWBER to write).
(enjoying this) It's Latin grammar now, sir. A look from DAVID: no it isn't!
(oh dear) Ah! Latin. Good. Conjugations! Active indicatives! Amo, amas,
amat. Aquarium, aquarius, gymnasium
And how does that ode continue?
In much the same vein, before reaching its apposite end. Or Terminus! To use the Latin word. (hastily) But we linger too long in the ancient world. Let us diversify.
MICAWBER turns to the blackboard, finds a space on which to write, picks up some chalk.
Behold, the most important numerical lesson a man can learn. Annual income twenty pounds...
He chalks 'PS20' on the board.
No, smarty-pantaloons. Result: happiness!
STEERFORTH begins grinning widely. The others follow suit. DAVID torn between his two friends.
But, conversely, annual income twenty pounds... (chalks 'PS20') ...annual expenditure twenty pounds nought and six... (chalks '20.0s.6d =') ...result - misery! Or at least until something turns up.
STEERFORTH turns wide-eyed and smiling to DAVID.
(mouths, points) It's him!
(mouths) Don't say anything...
Please copy that in your best hand.

The boys get to work. Silence. Close on DAVID. We hear the wheeze of a concertina being taken out of a bag. DAVID's eyes widen with horror. Then: terrible music. Everyone looks up. MICAWBER is playing. Nods to DAVID - "Great, isn't it?"
(over the music) There is a belief among a good many medical men that music may help in the absorption of knowledge.
MICAWBER plays on. Sniggers begin, then laughing.
Enough! I'm tempted to burst my ear drums with a pair of pencils.
I beg your pardon, sir?
Steerforth meant that possibly the instrument might have developed a leak, or...
Not at all. I meant he is a dismal musician.
Be quiet please, Mr Steelforge. Who are you to insult a gentleman...?
(heads to MICAWBER, looks around the room) Where is he, this gentleman? I see only an impudent beggar. Put that damned contraption down.

He tries to grab the concertina. MICAWBER holds on to it. Tiny squeaks as each refuses to let go. DAVID gets up - unsure of whom to help. Funny, but then STEERFORTH grabs the concertina and throws it violently across the room. MRS STRONG enters, with URIAH.
I heard some manner of mad wheezing
Professor. Is there a squirrel trapped in the pipes again?
He's no professor. Ask him about debtors' prison. Ask him about the scores of IOUs in Copperfield's trunk.
STEERFORTH looks to URIAH. The smallest of nods from URIAH.
'Scores' is an exaggeration. A few. Five or six...ten at most...
He's extorted money for years and has followed Daisy here to continue his efforts.
Mr Steepgorge is correct in that I did, to my shame, reside within prison walls after pecuniary...
Even we draw the line at employing former convicts! (realises...) ...and indeed, much higher than that is where we actually draw the line. We'll part, if you please. Mr Heep, show him out.
URIAH leads out MICAWBER. DAVID goes to object but MICAWBER places a hand on his shoulder.
It's no matter, my friend...
MICAWBER picks his concertina up, stuffs it, unlocked, in his bag, so it emits a small, muffled tuneless sigh as he walks.
EXT. CANTERBURY STREET - NEXT MORNING

DAVID runs through the streets. People boarding the coach, luggage stowed. DAVID sees the MICAWBERS. A pregnant MRS MICAWBER - wearing a cape made from a curtain, the curtain tie with tassels around her neck - sees him, gives him a hug.
No sooner are we reunited than we must part again. Like the Bible story. I'm sure there has to be a
Bible story where that happens.
(to MR MICAWBER) Were you aware I studied at Mrs Strong's before you came here?
Not as I recall. I had perhaps a dim awareness of the more recent chapters in your odyssey, but...
But your being here as a professor, in the same building as me...?
Pure kismet and happenstance. "As flies to wanton boys are we to the gods," to quote our ultimate poet.
All aboard who's going aboard!
MRS MICAWBER boards with the CHILDREN. MR MICAWBER puts a hand on DAVID's shoulder. It's fatherly.
I do wonder... I have found the funds to pay for my family's travel, but my own fare is lacking.
Behind MICAWBER is a chalked sign: 'Canterbury to London PS1'.
Might I possibly trouble you...
...no trouble...
...for the exact sum...
...of course...
DAVID takes out a pound note.
...of four pounds ten and thruppence?
MICAWBER clocks the PS1 sign, and makes a too-late attempt to cover it. He aborts the mission half way through putting his hand up. A weary beat from DAVID, who hands over the money.
Safe travels.
A beat of recognition, they both know what just happened. Then MICAWBER is back to his old self.
(climbing onto the coach, it pulls away) We are quadrilaterally concluded! In short, square!
(pleased) Geometry. Goodbye young sir! Until we meet again!
As the coach disappears DAVID sees URIAH across the street. Traffic keeps passing so they have to wait to speak.
I imagine you have forgotten!
Tea? No I... Traffic.
Why on earth should you remember people of our station? We have a nerve to expect it.
I haven't forgotten...

Traffic. Then when the traffic clears, URIAH is gone. DAVID, depressed, angry. Rips up MICAWBER's IOU. Turns to see the BUTCHER'S BOY and his ASSISTANT delivering meat from a handcart further down the street. DAVID, fired up, goes over. Pulls back his arm to thump the BUTCHER'S BOY, but immediately gets a shoulder of lamb in the face.
EXT. THE HEEPS' HOUSE - DAY
Establisher of the Heeps' humble home.
You say he hit you with a joint of meat?
INT. THE HEEPS' HOUSE - DAY

URIAH and his MOTHER in their sparsely furnished, but neat and clean house, sat on a large round table covered in tea things, including a bottle of milk. A pile of law books. The odd ornament. DAVID has a red mark on one side of his face.
A leg of lamb I believe. MRS HEEP (checking the mark) No. That looks more like a half shoulder. Or a big bit of brisket. (offering cake) Cake? It's quite heavy.
I like a heavy cake. I like to know I've had a cake.
He can't abide a light sponge.
I see... (takes a piece, struggles to cut into it) Well, this looks very... (eats - Jesus) ...good Lord. That's wonderfully dense.
MRS HEEP moves her chair closer to DAVID. A moment later, so does URIAH. DAVID uncomfortable. Looks around...
I see you're studying, Mr Heep?
Uriah, if you can bear to. Yes, improving my legal knowledge. I am hopeful that Mr Wickfield might be willing to take me on as an apprentice at law.
Perhaps you'll become a partner!
Do you mock me?
You seem to search for mockery. That was meant sincerely, Uriah.
(overexcited) "Uriah"! Did you hear that? He called me Uriah! I did!
Spontaneously!
And him a gentleman!
It's like the blowing of old breezes to hear you say Uriah. It thrills me to the very stomach.
(checking the clock) I'm happy for you.
Time is a concern? Are you worried humbleness is an infectious disease?
No, no, I just can't stay too long...
You can if I bar the door!
We could keep him as our little pet.
I beg your pardon?
URIAH realises he's gone too far.
Sorry, it's a joke! Forgive me, I've been attempting to learn Gentlemen's Humour from a book.
He has! What do you think of Mr Wickfield?
Both HEEPS shift chairs in exact unison closer to DAVID.
He is...a good man, I feel.
He takes wine with an enviable
degree of enjoyment, don't he?
I've seen him take wine, but...
You've seen him. That's good to know, interesting to know - that you've witnessed the deed.
'Witnessed the deed'?
URIAH and MRS HEEP nudge closer. This time they move the place settings they left behind with previous shifts.
Your associate, the Professor. He's a sort, isn't he? Is he?
Lodging with a beggar, is that a London particular?
Mr Micawber isn't a...
(off a look from URIAH) More tea?
MRS HEEP exits to the kitchen with the teapot. URIAH nudges even closer. A beat.
I had an interesting talk with Micawber as I was showing him out. Did you?
Fascinating, your time in London. (calling, clinking milk) Mother! Bring another bottle of milk through. This bottle is almost
empty and another bottle is needed.
You seem very vexed by this bottle.
Your friend Steerforth is damning of the humbler classes. A veritable factory of damnation. How would you like to help me secure a position with Mr Wickfield?
(gets close) I know you told Steerforth about Micawber's IOUs. I've a mind to throw this cake at you and break a rib.
You're very fond of violence, aren't you?
DAVID gets up to leave. MRS HEEP comes in with a tray of tea, milk, huge fruitcake.
More heavy cake!
Ahh! Lovely.
(to DAVID) You must take some home with you!
EXT. STREET OUTSIDE THE HEEP'S HOUSE - DUSK
DAVID leaves the Heep house, with a large slice of cake. He's angry. Throws the cake into a metal bucket with a massive clang. He strides back towards school with some determination.
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - SIDE OF BUILDING - DUSK

A little later. The mark on DAVID's face has gone down. DAVID storms up to STEERFORTH. Some hand-carts taking boys' luggage away. LITTIMER, Steerforth's Butler, carries out some of his bags through a side door.
Steerforth! Micawber's harmless. He cared for my well-being for years.
The rogue was exploiting your charitable nature. You should be thanking me Daisy.
My name is David. Not Daisy, not Trot, my name is David Copperfield.
Then why not go by it? What else are you hiding, David?
Well I can't hide anything when you've got your spindly little spy Heep searching my possessions.
My instinct is to protect you, because you can't protect yourself. Yes I can!
A malnourished apprentice knocked you out cold!
DAVID goes to hit STEERFORTH. BOYS watch from the windows.
What was that? It was like you were
reaching for a Chelsea bun. DAVID strikes STEERFORTH again, who instinctively hits DAVID back on the bottom, with his cane. DAVID yelps.
And that was like an ageing dowager poking a fire.
What does that even mean? You're just a bag of words.
What is it? What's in you that makes you like this?
I don't know. (drops arms to his sides) Go on. Hit me. I deserve to be hit.
I don't want to hit you.
Hit me. In the face. No!
I'm sorry. I'm forever doing this I make a dear friendship and then I tread it into the dirt...
No. We're still dear friends. We always shall be.
They sit on a trunk/bench.
Forgive me if I went too far with the Pretend Professor. Truly. It's upset you and I'm sorry. Thank you.
I'm fretting. And angry. Mother is due and I always get this odd feeling, which...I don't know...
...smoulders within you like smoke from damp logs. Yes.
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - FRONT - DUSK
MRS STEERFORTH - an imperious, well-dressed woman with an old scar on her lip - appears out of a carriage, head first, in SLOW MOTION. The wind catches her shawl.
INT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - CORRIDOR - DUSK

SLOW MOTION: MRS STEERFORTH strides through the hallway accompanied by a nervous MRS STRONG. LITTIMER holds an umbrella over MRS STEERFORTH to shield her from drips. She glares at the interior as she goes. They pass holes in the walls. There are BOYS behind sections of the wall holding it up, and covering holes with similarly coloured material. These are removed a moment after MRS STEERFORTH passes - we catch a glimpse of a LITTLE BOY's head through a hole, before it disappears. As they turn the corner, we return to a normal frame rate:
We intend, when further funds clear, to improve the building.
How? By demolishing it?
Haha. Very droll, Mrs Steerforth.
Any wit was unintentional. (glancing into a room) Someone has left a clarinet in that pantry.
That's actually the Music Room.
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - SIDE OF BUILDING - DUSK
Back with DAVID and STEERFORTH, now sat down.
If Micawber is real, does that mean the others from your stories are too? The factory boy?
Look, I can't do this any more. Now seems as good a time... James?
MRS STEERFORTH (O.S.)
MRS STEERFORTH and LITTIMER appear in the doorway. MRS STEERFORTH folds her umbrella, hands it to DAVID without looking at him.
Mother! This isDAVID David Copperfield. My very great pleasure to...
What is your background, Mr Copperfield? Who are your people? My people?
Your family. Are they anyone?
DAVID nervous under this scrutiny.
My parents died when...
Prep school?
Yes. In, um...in London.
I'll probably know it. Which one? Creakle's?
Creakle? Is that a saint? I don't think so. Who was the headmaster?
Mr Murdstone. Quick look to STEERFORTH to see if he's recognised the name.
What was the uniform?
Uh...trousers, certainly, and... MRS STEERFORTH is bored now...
(straightening STEERFORTH's collar) Anyway, James - I'm here. Since you bleated and bleated. I'll see you at Mrs Strong's interminable speech. I've brought a book. And a pillow.
She takes the umbrella from DAVID without a thank-you, exits
My mother.
I see. Good lord.
Did you notice her scar? As a young boy she once exasperated me. So I threw a hammer at her.
(thinks he's joking) Ha ha! (realises he isn't) Oh.
So, where did you school? And was Murdstone a master there?
There was no prep school. Ah.
My classroom was a bottling factory and my bed was two of Micawber's dining chairs. I'm here because my aunt saved me. Admirable.
Do you mean it?
Of course! Self-made man. Picked life up by the scruff and shook it. Thank you.
Kicked misfortune in the britches! May I still call you Daisy? No. Ha.
STEERFORTH
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - SCHOOL FIELD - DAY

The next day. MRS STRONG stands on a log on the lawn. The school is partly hidden behind a thick hedge. The leaving party looks very cheap. Lots of PARENTS and BOYS are heading for their carriages, through a gap in the hedge. WICKFIELD is with AGNES, who looks different, new dress and hairstyle.
(aware nobody is listening) ...as our reputation strengthens, so our intake of new boys for the coming year will be more select and exclusive than ever before.
STEERFORTH nudges DAVID, holds up three fingers. DAVID sniggers.
Thank you all for coming to Leavers' Day! (MORE)
Do stay for refreshments. We're delighted to- (as PEOPLE leave) -oh, good-bye!
Loads of PEOPLE continue to exit. MR WICKFIELD approaches URIAH and MRS HEEP, who watch AGNES. URIAH fills his glass.
(proud) My daughter, Agnes.
She's lovely.
MR WICKFIELD raises his glass to AGNES and wanders off.
Very pure. Skin like alabaster.
Mother, am I not growing too old for a bachelor?
BETSEY, with MR DICK, leads MR SPENLOW - prosperous, late 40s - towards DAVID, who is distracted by DORA, standing off to one side.
Trotwood! This is Mr Spenlow. Your future employer.
What am I to be? A Brazier!
A proctor. You know this Trot.
(distracted, pushing past) Yes. Sorry. Good afternoon, Mr Spenlow.
MR DICK was about to say something but DAVID's gone. MRS STRONG approaches with a tray of food.
Would you like a small sausage? Or lots of them? We have so many.
As SPENLOW heads off with BETSEY we see what's been distracting DAVID - DORA, the most beautiful woman he's ever seen. She's holding a small dog, JIP.
You were staring slightly. Is there something wrong with me?
No. Goodness me no. I apologise for my rudeness.
He is apologising Jip. Shall we forgive him? (nothing from JIP) He says we shall.
Thank you Jip.
(doing Jip's voice, low) Think nothing of it, sir.
He speaks very well.
It was actually me! I like to pretend he speaks. Some people think it idiotic.
They're next to an apple tree.
Oh, I do it myself, all the time. Don't I Mr Apple Tree? (tree's voice) Yes(loses confidence) -you do. Awkward beat.
I'm David Copperfield.
Are you still being the tree? No.
He offers his hand.
I'm Dora. Spenlow.
Spenlow! Dora Spenlow!
Yes, I don't know why I said it like that. "Dora. Spenlow." I don't usually stop in the middle.
They just stop and stare at each other. Then:
BETSEY TROTWOOD (O.S.)
DAVID heads off.
(as JIP) Good-bye sir!
Sorry. What was that?
Oh, it was...it was Jip.
Ah, yes! (as the tree) Good-bye! (points to the tree)
I know.
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - FRONT - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
STUDENTS are being congratulated by MRS STRONG, and collecting their Leavers Books. DAVID passes MRS STRONG.
Good luck Mr Copperfield. I hope this establishment has lived up to your expectations?
It is, I'm afraid, a crumbling disgrace. But I've been happy.
That's lovely to hear. Thank you.
DAVID hurries off. MRS STEERFORTH and STEERFORTH go over to MRS STRONG. She assumes they'll say something.
Don't speak.
MRS STEERFORTH shakes MRS STRONG's hand and brushes past. As does STEERFORTH.
It's an emotional time, I understand.
EXT. MRS STRONG'S SCHOOL - SCHOOL FIELD - DAY (CONTINUOUS)120
WICKFIELD walks with BETSEY. He has an empty wine glass. AGNES is nearby, talking to some TEACHERS, glancing occasionally at her father to make sure he's okay.
I should like to go home.
Just another two hours.
What if the donkeys are back?
Forty-five minutes, then.
WICKFIELD, a bit drunk, stumbles.
Very uneven ground here. I almost stumbled myself a moment ago.
Very dangerous indeed. There should be signs up.
URIAH approaches with a bottle of wine.
Some more wine sir?
No. I don't need it.
He doesn't need it. Thank you.
I see. You want me to take it away, sir? The delicious, tannic, deepred wine?
Yes please.
Actually - I will have the smallest amount. An ounce. Less.
HEEP fills WICKFIELD's glass, exits as MRS STRONG approaches.
Ah, Mr Wickfield, while I've got you cornered - not cornered but 'at bay', may I talk about roofs? And our ever-so-slight lack of them...?
BETSEY leaves them to talk as DAVID, excited, approaches with MR DICK.
(to BETSEY) Aunt - where's Agnes? I want to tell her something.
She's just there Trot. Looking very beautiful I think. Go speak to her.
DAVID heads off. DICK goes to follow but BETSEY holds him back, and steers him away.
(re kite) Useless. Without a breeze it's just wood and paper. Stupid kite.
It's not a stupid kite, Mr Dick. It's a splendid kite.
(mumbled) It's quite stupid.
DAVID nods an apology to the TEACHERS as he pulls AGNES away from them.
Agnes! (notices her new look) You look very striking.
Why thank you...
I am in love!
(hang on - with me?) You're in love...?
Utterly. With Dora. Spenlow. I don't know why I said it like that.
(right) Ah! The girl with the yapping dog.
What a face.
What a voice that comes out of it.
Do you mock me Agnes?
I do. With affection, but entirely without mercy. (DAVID now distracted) Do you know, Papa and I are also moving to London?
(looks for MR SPENLOW) I see...
He sees DORA showing an ARMY OFFICER JIP's trick.
You'll think I'm following you. (tickling DAVID) "Help me! I'm being followed by
Agnes!" She's as persistent as a bluebottle in a sash window!
DAVID's gaze still locked on to DORA.
As persistent as...a thing...can't think of the word... (sees SPENLOW) Sorry.
Leaving AGNES, he runs over to SPENLOW, BETSEY and MR DICK. Shakes SPENLOW's hand vigorously.
Mr Spenlow! Apologies for my earlier distracted state. I am looking forward tremendously, Mr Spenlow, to joining Spenlow & Jorkins. Mr Spenlow.
Excellent. Ready to become a proctor?
Eager to become a proctor!
(heading off) That's the attitude!
(to DAVID) What's a proctor?
EXT. LONDON COACHING INN - DAY
A busy, hectic coaching inn. Horses, parked coaches, kiosks. DAVID alights off a coach, looking grand: He owns this city.
I haven't the faintest idea....
EXT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - DAY
DAVID arriving outside his first apartment.
But I'll take possession of my own apartments.
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - DAY
DAVID looks out the window, sees ST PAUL'S. A modern, rich part of town. A few cranes in the distance.
..and soon I'll find out.
MRS CRUPP, his landlady, drags his trunk in. DAVID smiles a thank you. She doesn't move. He reaches into his pocket for some money.
I don't suppose you know what a proctor is?
Ooh. Now you're asking. Do they make hats?
EXT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
SPENLOW leads DAVID up King's Bench Walk, into No.3 North.
A proctor is a sort of monkish attorney. Our existence...
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
SPENLOW leads DAVID though the silent room. Busy CLERKS.
...in the natural course of things
SPENLOW hopscotches a dance over part of the floor. DAVID tries to emulate him, but treads on every squeaky floorboard. The squeaks are very loud and the CLERKS all look up angrily.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - DAY (A NEW DAY)
DAVID - with a new look to his hair - is listening to DORA's angelic singing (the last few bars of The Madman, 1846) from upstairs.
Oi - do some work, Romeo.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - STAIRWELL - DAY (LATER) 127
As DORA leaves, DAVID skips across the creaky floorboards, trying to avoid squeaks, but failing. CLERKS mutter.
Miss Spenlow, I was convinced a famous soprano was practicing upstairs!
(excited) Oh! Exciting. Who?
No, I mean to say...it turned out to be you!
Oh dear. How disappointing for you. No, I-
Oh! No, I see! A compliment! Thank you!
Not at all.
I am relieved to hear that my voice is not tiresome.
'Not tiresome' is an understatement. And 'angelic' is not an overstatement.
Oh, thank you! Again.
Not at all! Again. (awkward pause) You have just come home from Paris, I believe? Yes.
Paris. What a city.
Have you ever been there? No.
I hope you'll go soon.
DAVID laughs. Doesn't know why he laughed.
I won't go to Paris. I won't leave England under any circumstances while you...
Copperfield! Please search the birth records of Putney for one Jemima Poole. She was born some time last century.
I have to go.
He begins to step back across the squeaky floor.
Would you like to come up after my next lesson? I can sing you a
piece.
I will bring something to throw at you. As in a bouquet. Of flowers.
DORA smiles, exits. DAVID skips back across the floorboards, and makes slightly fewer squeaks. CLERKS still mutter.
Did you get your shoes fitted at a blacksmith's?
INT. TAILOR'S SHOP - DAY

MONTAGE: With DORA singing Woodman Spare That Tree, played on the forte piano. Quick cuts of DAVID choosing, trying on, buying various items of clothing. All are bright, garish, dandyish, colourful. Looking in a mirror, helped by a TAILOR.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - DAY
DAVID getting better at the floorboards, even while balancing a big pile of ledgers. Just one SQUEAK at the end.
Fell at the last fence. Sadly going to have to shoot you.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICE - UPSTAIRS ROOM - DAY
Intercut with moments of listening to DORA singing. DAVID following every syllable, nodding along, lightly swaying to it, maybe a single tear forming. Her dog, JIP, playing tricks.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICE - DOWNSTAIRS - DAY
DAVID watches DORA leaving from one side of a large central chimney. He hurries to the other side of the chimney to watch the second half of her journey. Avoids all the squeaky boards.
That's what I want to hear nothing.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - STAIRWELL - DAY

DAVID presenting DORA with flowers and bows as she comes down the stairs. She makes him a small origami flower in return. INTERCUT WITH: Small vignettes of awkward conversation with DORA, getting slightly more confident:
EXT. LONDON - CHEQUER SQUARE - DAY
DAVID and DORA walking. Her hat blows off, DAVID leaps and retrieves it, hands it back to her.
Lovers have loved before but no lovers shall ever love as we love.
That's very complicated, but thank you.
EXT. LONDON STREETS - CHEQUER SQUARE - EVENING

DAVID walks home. The clouds seem to form into DORA's face. All the adverts on the side of an omnibus say 'DORA'. An inn has a painting of DORA on its sign: 'THE DORA'S HEAD'. A CHIMNEY SWEEP bumps into someone, drops his brushes. On the ground, the broken up poles and heads spell 'DORA'. A COACH passes by: the COACH DRIVER has Dora's ribbon and curls.
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - NIGHT
DAVID stares at St Paul's dome. It has DORA's curls and ribbon. Behind him, young men shouting, laughing, singing. The moon shines bright. The SONG ends.
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - NIGHT - LATER
A roar of laughter. DAVID is having a party with STEERFORTH, MARKHAM, and his Oxford friend GRAINGER. It's smokey, raucous and everyone is tipsy. We're at the start of dinner.
I never saw such curls! How could I, for there never were such curls!
MRS CRUPP appears with a large piece of mottled re-formed meat. Plonks it down.
Now I know this thing resembles a slab of marble, but(to MRS CRUPP) -I'm assured it is what they term 'Mock Turtle'? Nothing from MRS CRUPP.
Look at the size of it!
DAVID confident, a man about town.
Could you warm the Mock Turtle up please, Mrs Crupp?
You want me to warm this up? ...yes.
She gestures for the meat, and STEERFORTH passes it over. With a huff, MRS CRUPP heaves the meat over her shoulder, grumbling as she staggers into the pantry.
(re wine) A rather extensive order, eh Markham?
Enough for a decent headache, certainly.
They look so numerous I am almost frightened by them. (counts the bottles) Two missing.
DAVID sees MRS CRUPP's shadow cast from the pantry onto a wall. The shadow uncorks a bottle, then takes a long swig.
What's the grape? And the vintage? DAVID's confidence drains. STEERFORTH spots this. DAVID studies the label. It's in French and the words become jumbled.
(pouring) The merest sip should give you these answers.
GRAINGER sips. He has no more idea than DAVID.
...Ah yes. Unmistakeable.
(quietly victorious) Quite so.
(knocks back a full glass) It's a red wine. Almost certainly.
HUGE LAUGHS. DAVID getting drunk to get his confidence back. Snacking on quails eggs and celery salt.
I'm starving.
MRS CRUPP reappears, also quite drunk, with a grubby pan containing a liquid slop: the Mock Turtle now a shrunken, bleak nub.
Where's the rest of it?
You gentlemen tuck in. I'm full.
Is this a burnt offering to the pagan gods, Daisy? Hoist it high!
DAVID stabs the meat with a fork, holds it above his head.
I shall have a dinner party like this once a week until I die! Where are the lobsters? I want lobster!
CRUPP doesn't move, stands drunk and swaying.
If you'll just give me two minutes.
I am very familiar with the lobster people of Yarmouth.
Do they worship lobsters, or take on their characteristics?
They're hardworking toilers of the sea.
The lobsters, or the people? Both!
MRS CRUPP wanders off.
If you're thinking of travelling to Yarmouth soon, might I join you? Of course.
It would be fun to be part of that world. I love to sail, and fish. In the city I can be prone to a heavy mind.
(finds this hilarious) You have a heavy mind?
He bangs his head on the table. A big laugh from the others.
Sometimes - yes, like lead. A lead head. I feel my...
The moment is interrupted by a CRASH! A bottle has smashed.
Sorry! My fault! I'll lick the wine

up and try to avoid the glass... DAVID looking in the mirror. In the background we see MRS CRUPP is sat with the boys, singing a maudlin song to the tune of 'Old 1812'. MARKHAM is trying to harmonize.
Westminster is full of wigs, Lawyers heads, and briefs and bags, Lords and Commons, carts and gigs, Silks and satins, rogues and rags,
Covent Garden, Drury Lane,
STEERFORTH on his own, bored. GRAINGER eating some gravy and
I am very ill. And my hair looks drunk. (calling to the others) I say! I have drunken hair. (to himself) Drunken hair.
DAVID stares. Faints out of shot. STEERFORTH lifts him.
Up! I'm bored up to my eyeballs! Let's give your drunken hair a night to remember. To the theatre!
They all get up to go to the theatre. MRS CRUPP thinks she's coming. But the door is closed on her. Then DAVID pops his head back in.
(v. politely) Could you tidy up? Thank you.
INT. LONDON THEATRE - SECOND TIER BOX - NIGHT
It's dark. A play is on: 'BLACK-EYED SUSAN' (see addendum for content). DAVID and the boys tumble into a box, shushing each other. DAVID peers at his ticket.
Is this Box 12. Or 14? Or - it can't be Box 120 can it? That would make this an enormous theatre.
He gets to the front row and peers over the edge. AUDIENCE MEMBERS turn disapprovingly towards them.
I can't hear. Speak up! (re play dialogue) Who's Susan? I don't know who this Susan is. Stop saying Susan! Silence, please.
He is being silence please, so hold your tongue!
DAVID looks down to the first tier. In a corner box, is AGNES. Some other people are with her - we can't see the faces, but a hint it could be MR WICKFIELD, HEEP and MRS HEEP.
(very loud) Agnes! Ha haa! Good lord.
Trotwood! Please! Lower your voice.
Another AUDIENCE MEMBER looks towards AGNES.
AUDIENCE MEMBER 2
I'll thank you not to hush me, madam! Hush your own loud hushing.
Don't huss Agnesh. She's sort of my kind of sister in a sort of way.
So you do have a sister!
MARKHAM and GRAINGER laugh. The whole audience now hate DAVID. AGNES indicates - meet outside. She exits, and so does DAVID, clambering over chairs.
INT. LONDON THEATRE - HALLWAY OUTSIDE BOXES - NIGHT
DAVID and AGNES. DAVID trying to appear sober. AGNES has lost a spark, is troubled and tense.
Trotwood...
I'm terribly not drunk.
If I know one thing it's what a drunken man looks like. Did Steerforth get you into this state?
No he didn't. I mean, he was there while it was happening, but...
I fear he's a bad influence.
The only person who suffers from Steerforth's influence is himself...oh hello!
Suddenly STEERFORTH, MARKHAM and GRAINGER are there. They head past, towards the bar.
This is a very poor play. I wanted a play about ghosts or a murder.
Or a horse.
(to AGNES) Hello sis!
They go into the bar.
You were saying...?
Please don't lecture me about Steerforth.
A lecture would be futile - in your current state you barely understand English.
Let's not argue, please.
I haven't the energy to argue. I've barely the energy to...
WICKFIELD, URIAH - dressed very smartly - and MRS HEEP come out into the hallway.
Mister Copperfield! Look at us from Canterbury to London, a reverse pilgrimage!
(finds this hilarious) Oh very good...
Good evening everyone.
My dear Trotwood! Good evening. (to MRS HEEP) Did I fall asleep at one point? Are we all still understanding the play
do we think? 'Susan', etcetera?
Agnes, have you told him of my new position?
A beat. DAVID looks to AGNES.
Uriah has joined the firm. He has some very promising ideas with regards to our future prosperity...
Ury couldn't be making faster progress if he was steam-powered.
Mother, please - I redden in the face.
The HEEPS head to the bar with WICKFIELD. An awkward beat between AGNES and DAVID.
They live with us now. I hear their snores, like... love-lorn toads calling across a swamp.
(half a beat) And how's Dora?
Wonderful. Sweet. Curly. In fact, I propose to intend to marriage her in the morning.
(hides her deflation) What pleasing news.
(peering from the bar) Agnes - a soda water?
She nods a thank you as STEERFORTH, MARKHAM and GRAINER head out from the bar, and leave the theatre.
(to AGNES, but looking at his departing friends) Be very careful of the Heep and his cake-mother.
They're becoming a fixture. Don't follow your companions Trot, you need to go to bed. Good night.
She heads back into the box. The HEEPS and WICKFIELD are returning too - WICKFIELD draining a glass of wine, MRS HEEP replacing it immediately with a full one.
Very generous, but I may be nearing the limits of my capacity.
It'll be soaked up by my dense sponge.
(to URIAH) Wait...you called her Agnes, just now, not Miss Wickfield...
I must get back to the dramatics.
URIAH heads back in. DAVID left standing on his own. He feels very dizzy.
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - BEDROOM - NIGHT

Now DAVID is face-down in bed. Drifting in and out of drunken sleep, and dribbling. DAVID'S POV: Suddenly looming over him is URIAH HEEP, between the railings. The moon shines brightly through an open window behind URIAH. The floor is covered in scraps of paper, DAVID's notes and ideas.
Early moon. Peaceful, ain't she?
(startled awake) What?
Your landlady let me in. I thought you might need some help. And I love to help.
You called Agnes by her Christian name, not Miss Wickfield...
Did I? Too fast? I can wait. (leans closer to DAVID, staring through the bars of the bed) Ever tried to pluck a pear before it was ripe? They all ripen in the end. They only want attending to.
DAVID tries to grab URIAH, misses.
You're not worthy of that woman. (slightly incoherent) I hold Agnes so far above you and your aspirations as that moon herself.
I have as good a right to her as any other man. Better! I pulled myself up, with no help from you, doing whatever it is...what is it you're being trained to become? A proctor.
Indeed. Now, I'm hardly whatever one of them is, but I deserve her. I will go to any ends for her!
(inaudible mumble) You may go to the devil!
He falls on to the bed, passes out. URIAH puts the cushion on his seat, rests.
Don't say that! I know you'll be sorry afterwards!
(barely audible) I'm a proctor...
We are like two carved figures in a
weather house, Copperfield; as one
arrives, the other departs. David is asleep - CUT TO BLACK. Then...
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - BEDROOM - MORNING

DAVID wakes, bleary-eyed, hungover. Hauls himself upright ouch, he has a headache, falls back on the bed. Gathers himself, staggers up, lunges for some trousers, lies back down on the bed, tries to put on his trousers lying down. Some QUICK CUTS of him dressing - in pain from his headache as he leans down to pull on his socks, having to lie back on the bed and stick his legs in the air to pull them on. Goes to put an engagement ring in his waistcoat pocket. Drops it on the floor. Can't find it. Then can. Bends down to pick it up. Stands up too quickly, staggers and falls back on the bed.
EXT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - DAY
Establisher. We hear DORA's (very good) singing.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - UPSTAIRS ROOM - DAY
DAVID, dressed garishly, hungover and looking nauseous, is listening, spellbound, as DORA finishes singing "The Madman", accompanied by a PIANIST. She finishes, and he applauds.
Do you like my voice, Doady? I'm going to call you Doady. Do you mind?
I love being called by other names.
He takes a breath, hand goes in his waistcoat pocket to grab the ring.
And... in return... I have a new name it is my heart's desire to offer you...
Oh, no, I like 'Dora'. It reminds me of doors, and doors are such jolly and useful things! Do you earn a lot of money working for Papa?
Takes his hand out his pocket again - not the right moment.
I'm currently articled, training to be a proctor. So I pay him.
That doesn't sound right. But I don't fully understand money. It's all nonsense isn't it?
(not really, no) Yes.
Jip likes you. Don't you, Jip? (does Jip's voice) Indeed I do. (back to normal) Jip never lies.
Dora, may I be frank?
I don't like hearing frank expressions. Say it like Jip. Really?
DORA nods.
(attempting a Jip voice) I am intoxicated with joy whenever I see you. I love you. (own voice) Can I...?
Yes. That just seemed odd.
I idolize and worship you. If you would like me to die for you, say the word, and I am ready.
No, don't die. If you were dead you'd miss Jip's new trick. Look.
JIP stands for a nano-second/rolls over. DAVID charmed.
Dora, I'd like to ask you, if...
SPENLOW approaches, coming up the stairs.
Message from your landlady: "A man with a kite and a severe lady have arrived and need to see you now."
SPENLOW peers in, expecting DAVID to follow. He heads out.
You would like to ask me if...
If you would wait for my question later.
DAVID reluctantly follows SPENLOW.
EXT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - DAY
DAVID runs home.
EXT. LONDON - CHEQUER SQUARE - DAY
DAVID walks briskly through London streets. More squalid than ever. BAILIFFS' carts are being loaded with furniture. More HOMELESS PEOPLE sleeping on the streets.
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - LIVING ROOM - DAY

DAVID arrives home, slightly irritated and sweaty. BETSEY and MR DICK are in the living room, surrounded by their luggage. MR DICK clutches his kite to him. There is tea and toast on a table, and BETSEY's huge VASE from earlier. BETSEY is clearing up dozens of bottles and dirty glasses from David's party. DAVID begins to help with the clean-up.
(avoiding eye-contact) Trotwood, I am ruined. MR DICK Like a castle.
So Mr Dick suggested we come here.
Ruined? How can you be ruined?
Because I've lost everything Trot. In the mining way and the banking way. We've had to close up the house, say farewell to lovely Janet and walk away from our beautiful garden.
The green will become a paradise for donkeys.
I have now only my clothes, and a picture or two. And Mr Dick.
As they speak, we see projected on to the walls: BETSEY's
cottage (Sc 131A). MR DICK and BETSEY leave the house with
their bags as some PEOPLE on DONKEYS come across the green. BETSEY is unable to do anything about it.
My shares have plummeted like lead droppings from a stone goose.
(realising it's serious) No, no, this can't happen. I won't let all the light and goodness that you've brought me turn to gloom, not like it did before...
We're not the only ones to suffer. Two bailiffs' carts in this one
street alone...
She gestures out the window: two hand carts and a horse loaded with possessions, protesting/crying DEBTORS, dismissive BAILIFFS.
Surely Mr Wickfield has been monitoring your affairs?
Mr Wickfield has troubles of his own just now.
He very often sleeps during the day.
Because of... (mimes drinking, with a wine bottle, to MR DICK)
BETSEY for once looks at him with disfavour. He has accidentally shown her a version of himself he keeps for Steerforth, and she doesn't like it.
(very cross) I've no notion what that spasmodic gesture indicates. But if we're speaking of over-indulgence... (indicates the sea of empty bottles) (MORE)
Have you returned to the bottling business of your youth?
I entertained some friends...
...who seem to number the entire population of the city.
BETSEY takes the bottles outside. MR DICK left with DAVID, who paces back and forth, troubled.
Mr Dick, can you cast any more light on what has happened?
Well, the day before yesterday she said, "Dick, I am ruined." And I said, "Oh, indeed!" And then we travelled here and had bottled porter and sandwiches.
(frustrated with MR DICK) That's not a lot of light. Do you understand what ruin means?
MR DICK smiles and nods. Then stops smiling, shakes his head.
It means distress, and want. And starvation.
DAVID immediately regrets what he said. MR DICK starts taking pieces of buttered toast and putting them in his pockets.
Oh dear. What can we do, Trotwood? (to himself) "By what earthly power do you condemn me? By what authority?"
We'll get your kite in the air Mr Dick, and banish any sad thoughts to the skies. Agreed? Now, let's try to keep a cheerful countenance.
Agreed. Cheerful.
BETSEY enters. MR DICK tries a cheerful countenance while putting some sugar cubes in his breast pocket.
EXT. BETSEY TROTWOOD'S HOUSE - DAY

[NOTE: To be projected over a section of the previous scene.] BETSY's old cottage. MR DICK and BETSEY leave the house with their bags on hand carts as some PEOPLE on DONKEYS come across the green. BETSEY is unable to do anything about it.
EXT. WICKFIELD & HEEP OFFICES - DAY
Establisher of the offices. A sign reads 'WICKFIELD & CO'.
INT. WICKFIELD & HEEP OFFICES - DAY
DAVID is opposite MR WICKFIELD, who sits behind his desk. A subdued AGNES enters the room carrying a tray covered with a cloth, which she sets down.
We will ensure this cannot happen again. Although that's problematic, given I've no idea how it happened in the first place - I'd never authorise such a reckless document.
(gently) And yet...this is your signature.
You did all you could. Currently we all have to do the dutiful thing.
What does that mean? Do you feel some personal duty towards Uriah?
No answer from AGNES.
I can offer you a crumb of comfort, Trotwood. In fact more than a crumb, an entire batch loaf - we'll approve a loan to tide you over...
Thank you. And I promise repayment will occur just as soon as...
'Something turns up'?
URIAH has entered, holding a square object wrapped in cloth and a DISTINCTIVE BRIEFCASE, which he then locks in a bureau.
(locking the bureau) With respect, Mr Wickfield, a more sober judgement is required when considering a loan of this nature.
I am sober.
Mast...Mister Copperfield, a loan is out of the question. Apologies.
(getting up) Mr Heep, we should discuss this...
Do you have authority here, Uriah?
(sits in WICKFIELD's seat) I do. And it's Mr Heep. As in 'Wickfield & Heep'. I'm a partner!
He reveals a new PLAQUE from beneath the cloth: 'WICKFIELD & HEEP'. 'HEEP' is a font-size bigger.
Agnes? Could you...?
AGNES uncovers the tray, which has a decanter of sherry and some glasses. WICKFIELD feigns enthusiasm.
We are drinking to the firm hand of
Mr Heep grasping - or co-grasping the tiller.
All of us, man and woman, can benefit from having a partner, do you not agree Agnes?
In times of trouble we must all do...that which we must do.
AGNES starts to pour. MRS HEEP enters with a tray of cakes.
Small heavy cakes to go with the sherry! Like tasty billiard balls.

MR WICKFIELD goes to take a sherry. A look from URIAH to AGNES and she takes it off him. Takes a sherry to DAVID. URIAH and MRS HEEP drink and celebrate, marveling at the new sign. MR WICKFIELD remains in his seat away from the desk. DAVID whispers to AGNES.
(re URIAH & MRS HEEP) Those two are weeds. I've seen their like before. Unchecked, they'll overrun and choke all life and joy from this place. They must be stopped.
I fear the time for that has passed. Now we must all make what shift we can.
URIAH proposes a toast.
To partnerships!
He crosses to AGNES, gets very close. MRS HEEP gives DAVID her mini cakes.
I've given you two cakes. I know how you love my cake. Cake.
I'm far too humble to say I've saved this firm, but... A beat.
...Uriah has been very good for our business.
He has been diversifying into smaller rental properties...
...in parts of London that aren't necessarily first choice.
And this is to be my only choice?
There is a category of persons who famously cannot be choosers.
INT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - LIVING ROOM - DAY
BETSEY and MR DICK in David's lodgings surrounded by luggage.
It is into this category that your
aunt currently falls. They're pushed closer together as the walls close in and the apartment shrinks. BETSEY grabs the huge VASE. Janet!
And you with her. Quickly packing before they lose their belongings to the
walls. Last thing to be grabbed is MR DICK's kite.
Now this property is maybe not the most spacious of city abodes... The room has now turned into...
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - LIVING ROOM - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

DAVID and URIAH are with BETSEY and MR DICK in the much smaller, scruffier set of rooms. DAVID's stuff in boxes, piles of his notes everywhere. DICK and BETSEY sit on their luggage, beside the GIANT VASE. BETSEY hangs the WINDCHIMES (from earlier) on a nail.
It will suit someone in your circumstances very well indeed.
I am not someone in my circumstances. Of course.
At least we won't get lost in here.
There isn't room enough to swing a cat.
Good. I don't want to swing a cat.
BETSEY gives DICK a little smile.
You can't fly your kite in here!
I don't fly it indoors anyway. It can't fly in a house. No breeze.
I bow to your expertise.
He can't fly his kite, but he could swat an irritant.
Well, enjoy your lodgings. If any problems arise, I suggest you tend to them yourselves.
He exits. MR DICK tries to stretch, but can't.
We'll make do. Ale for me now. It's a great deal better than wine anyway. Not half so bilious.
I wish to make a contribution.
MR DICK puts out a handkerchief, dumps it into BETSEY's lap. Some coins, buttons, bits of string, marbles and sweets spill out. BETSEY is moved. DAVID still pacing.
I'd have been shut up to lead a dismal life these many years, but you took me in, like David's friend took his family in, in Yarmouth...
Wait! Yarmouth? What's today?
(confidently) I know this. It's Wednesday.
It's Tuesday, Trot.
Tuesday! I should be meeting Steerforth to take him to Yarmouth. (really wants to get away) I can postpone. Easily.
(gently taps DICK's hand) (MORE)
Meanwhile Mr Dick and I will try to make this the most desirable
mousehole in London.
DICK leans over as if to put his hand on hers, but touches the kite instead. Quick JUMP CUTS of DAVID hurriedly packing two bags.
EXT. LONDON - EXCHANGE ALLEY - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
DAVID rushes down an alleyway with two travel bags.
EXT. LONDON - LONG LANE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
DAVID runs through the lane with two travel bags.
EXT. LONDON - BISHOPS LANE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
DAVID runs through a market, with two travel bags.
Mr Copperfield? I'm your butcher. About our outstanding invoice...
But DAVID does the old MICAWBER trick of walking in step with moving coaches to cross the street without being spotted. The BUTCHER looks for him in vain.
EXT. BOTTLING WAREHOUSE - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
DAVID passes the entrance to the BOTTLING WAREHOUSE. Some BOYS and GIRLS stare out at him as he hurries past. These include the 4-YEAR-OLD DAVID, but dressed much shabbier than before.
EXT. DAVID'S FIRST LODGINGS - DAY (CONTINUOUS)

STEERFORTH checks his watch, waiting. DAVID approaches STEERFORTH from the side without being spotted, hidden behind a horse pulling a cart full of building material and ladders: using the Micawber trick we saw in scene 55.
Apologies for lateness. I've just been in my apartment. In there. STEERFORTH just has the one bag.
Two bags! Such extravagance given
how much the coach people charge.
Ah, no - this contains laundry for Mrs Crupp. I said I'd leave it here for her to collect.
He puts it on the step.
Curious arrangement. But we should get to the Spread Eagle, our coach is due to leave in 10 minutes.
They head off. DAVID looks back as a HOMELESS MAN picks up his bag, walks off with it.
EXT. YARMOUTH BEACH - DUSK
Cold, windy. DAVID and STEERFORTH walk with a lantern.
What might appear as rough charms were magical to me as a child...
What a delightful residence.
In my recollection it was more colourful.
Nonsense! Every colour in the rainbow jostles for our attention.
INT. BOATHOUSE - DUSK (CONTINUOUS)
DAVID and STEERFORTH enter. It's small, smelly, dilapidated, cluttered. Low ceiling. DAVID immediately deeply embarrassed. A big cheer from PEGGOTTY, DANIEL PEGGOTTY and HAM. Davy!
She hugs and kisses him.
(shaking hands) Look at you! Growed out of all knowledge! You in good kelter boy?
(struggling to know which voice to use) Good kelter. Aye. Arr. Yes. (MORE)
I'm very well thank you. This is my dear friend James Steerforth.
Ah, right...
This young man's affection for you is such that I feel I know you all. (shaking hands) Mr Peggotty, I presume.
Alright then. And HamYes.
HAM
-and Peggotty. An honour to meet you.
(charmed) You have a lovely speaking voice.
That's a very bright waistcoat.
It's brocade. Savile Row.
(to the others, knowledgable) That's a street in London.
(re DAVID) We mardled plenty with this 'un when he was a nipper and we're uncommon proud of him, thankee.
That's dialect, isn't it? I'm fascinated by how language changes around the country.
We can write it down for you if it's too difficult. We can write.
An awkward laugh from STEERFORTH. DAVID senses tension from HAM, tries to move things on.
Ham and Mr Peggotty are expert lobster catchers.
Do you trawl or use pots?
(trying to help) I think probably...trawling? Pots. Or pots.
Wire and wood, or netting? HAM Bit of net, bit of wood, bit of wire. Old Mr Lobster wanders in to eat in the kitchen, we catch him in the parlour.
Well I'm sure you are very polite to the fellow when both you meet!
There are some boiled lobsters in a pot.
We try to be sir! Save for the boiling, eh!? (pulls lobster out) Because we boils 'em, you see!
DAVID steps back and BANGS his head.
Mind your head!
Steerforth, come and look through here. It's the perfect little bedroom I told you about.
DAVID pulls the curtain to what was his whitewashed room. The whitewash is damp and peeling, the room is dirty, and there in the bed is MRS GUMMIDGE, coughing up phlegm into a bowl.
Oh, I'm so sorry.
PEGGOTTY arrives.
Mrs Gummidge ain't well. She brings more up than she eats, these days.
They say it can't be done. But I does it.
My apologies, Mrs Gummidge.
Oh, it don't signify. I'll be dead soon, so please the Lord.
They head back into the boat. DAVID bangs his head hard.
Mind your head!
Watch your head, Davy!

PEGGOTTY, HAM and DANIEL go and deal with MRS GUMMIDGE's sick. EMILY enters. She seems worn out, older than her years. She smiles at DAVID, then STEERFORTH. Embarrassed that she isn't better dressed and that her hands are a bit herring-y. She clasps her hands behind her back.
It's good to see you again Davy. And to meet you, sir. Ah! Emily?
He offers a hand to shake. EMILY keeps her hands behind her back, curtseys instead.
Daisy here tells me you climb the masts of sailing boats.
You can see for miles from up there. You can see all the ladies off to the ball at Browston Manor.
(going over to STEERFORTH) Did you just call Davy 'Daisy'? Is that dialect?
DANIEL PEGGOTTY comes back from MRS GUMMIDGE, who we hear coughing up mucus, then...
We've no food so don't expect food.
We do have some food. Fish.
STEERFORTH laughs.
I imagine you and Ham will have been married for some years now. No. No. Oh.
Still engaged. Still not married.
We've been saving up though, haven't we? And deciding where we want to live...
We want to live here. Knock up our own boathouse, couple of hundred yards up the coast.
Or...we could go further?
Of course we could. Half a mile, three-quarters even.
Listen to them. Lovebirds.
Peggotty! I fear I'm going to be sick in a substantial way.
No matter, our floor is a beach!

PEGGOTTY hurries past. DAVID moves aside, bangs his head again. We hear MRS GUMMIDGE heaving. A look between STEERFORTH and EMILY, both as distressed as each other by life in the boat. PEGGOTTY comes back out to get clean linen.
(to DAVID) Now, mind your head. DAVID bangs it again.
You both staying at the Star Hotel?
I am. I hear it is very good.
Very grand. But very expensive. Expensive?
Of course you're welcome to a hammock here but I imagine you'd...
I'd love a hammock here.
PEGGOTTY smiles, hugs DAVID, who steps back and bangs his head again. Then PEGGOTTY heads back to the noisy GUMMIDGE.
EXT. YARMOUTH HARBOUR SIDE - DAY
STEERFORTH gutting herring with all the women of the town, including EMILY and PEGGOTTY. Doing brilliantly. He's very quick at it. HAM watches.
Look at him...
Faster than me!
That's very good knife work, Mr Steerforth.
Thank you. I could be a murderer!
PEGGOTTY and EMILY laugh loudly. STEERFORTH grins.
EXT. BOATHOUSE - DAY
STEERFORTH repairing the BOATHOUSE. He's roped in a lot of other BOATMEN to help. DANIEL watches, pleased.
That stern looks like new, my friend. Splendid. Have you made fast the chimney there? Excellent! Good work, mates!
INT. YARMOUTH PUB - EVENING
A packed pub. STEERFORTH just finishing a story. DAVID at the bar watching him.
...so the hotel manager turns to me
and says... HAM and EMILY join DAVID.
"I've had thirty guests use that towel before you sir, and you're the first one to complain that it's dirty!"
Everyone's fond of your friend, eh Davy? Life and soul, isn't he?
Massive laugh from the crowd. STEERFORTH grins.
You seem to mean that with sarcasm Ham, but I know it to be true...
Ham, stop snipping and sniping like one of your lobsters. Can we go?
Another round of drinks for my friends!
See - what a benevolent gentleman.
Daisy will do the honours - won't you Daisy?
(oh dear) Of course.
HAM gives him and EMILY a smile.
Why are we always here anyway? What about the Anchor in Gorleston?
Who wants to go all the way to Gorleston? What's wrong with here?
Everyone is now heading over to get their drinks, crowding round the bar, yelling orders. DAN PEGGOTTY is among them.
Two pork pies Bob, one for me and then the other one for me after that.
(to BARMAN) Would you take an IOU? (off his look) No, of course not.

The BARMAN shakes his head. DAVID gives him the last of his money. Sees STEERFORTH suddenly left alone on the other side of the pub. Leaves the crowd, heads over. Out of the spotlight, STEERFORTH is down and deflated.
Ham and Emily have just been saying the same as me - that everyone loves James Steerforth!
Everyone except James Steerforth, my friend. (he downs a glass of rum) Did you have enough cash for the drinks? Plenty.
Are you sure? Yes, I...
(interrupting) Promise me Daisy, won't you, that you'll think of me at my best?
What do you mean?
Whatever might happen, please promise me that.
Of course. You seem low - where's this sudden cloud come from?

Oh, it hovers over me from time to time, glowering... (seemingly sincere) I suppose I'm just, as they say around these parts (exaggerated accent) "All in a jiffle like a spizzard up a crowpipe." Big, big laugh from DAVID.
(joining in) "Lolloping down the..."
(interrupting) Sorry, I don't mean to insult these people - your people.
They're not necessarily my...
But STEERFORTH has jumped up, full of energy again.
(loud, to the pub) Who's up for singing a shanty!? Cheer from the crowd at the bar.
(sings) One Friday morn when we set sail, Not very far from land, We there did spy a pretty maid... EVERYONE joins in.
(sings) With a comb and a glass in her hand, hand, hand, A comb and a glass in her hand...
INT. BOATHOUSE - DAY - LATE AFTERNOON
DAVID's hammock is rigged. PEGGOTTY is pulling down a gauze.
There you go. You can have some privacy.
Well, that's all I do have at the moment.
I know. But you had nothing, then you had something, now you've got nothing again. So stands to reason you'll have something again.
I wish I could be so sure it worked like that.
I could let you have... No, no...
It wouldn't be...
I couldn't possibly...
You're sure now? Half a beat.
Well, if it was...
The moment is interrupted as DANIEL PEGGOTTY enters.
You seen all that herring out there, with its guts still intact? What's Emily been up to?
HAM runs in with an envelope. In the background, we might spot Steerforth's HAT and CANE, left on the side.
Emily's written you a note, Uncle. It was in the cart.
DANIEL PEGGOTTY takes the envelope, opens it, reads. As he reads, images (scenes 148A & B) play out on the wall behind him, as if projected.
"When you, who love me better than I deserve, read this, I shall have gone far away, and won't come back unless he brings me back a lady."
Emily. And Steerforth. They'll be in the boat he rented!
He rushes out. DANIEL PEGGOTTY follows. PEGGOTTY and DAVID follow.
EXT. YARMOUTH BEACH - BOATHOUSE - AFTERNOON
[NOTE: To be projected on the inner wall of the boathouse, over Daniel Peggotty reading out her letter.] EMILY and STEERFORTH walk away from the boathouse, with their bags.
EXT. YARMOUTH HARBOUR SIDE - SMALL BOAT - AFTERNOON/EVENING
EMILY and STEERFORTH hurry through the quiet, empty harbour side to board the small boat, and begin their journey.
EXT. YARMOUTH BEACH - EVENING
HAM, PEGGOTTY, DANIEL PEGGOTTY. DAVID runs to catch up. In the very far distance at sea is a boat.
EXT. SMALL BOAT - EVENING
POV: STEERFORTH and EMILY, from behind, looking to DAVID, PEGGOTTY, HAM and DANIEL PEGGOTTY on the distant beach.
They all look so small. STEERFORTH puts his arm around her.
EXT. YARMOUTH BEACH - EVENING
DANIEL PEGGOTTY in shock. DAVID ashamed, close to tears. PEGGOTTY weeping. HAM furious, trying to contain his rage.
Emily! Emily jump! Swim to me! HAM runs to the water, he's about to wade in, but DAVID restrains him.
Ham, don't - in the dark - these currents - it's not safe...
DANIEL PEGGOTTY is mumbling. PEGGOTTY goes to comfort him.
This won't make you a lady my love.
I'll find her. I'll go find her, bring her back.
Where will you search?
Ill search everywhere... The world. Wherever she is, I'm going to find her. I'm nothing without her.
You and me, Ham. You won't go without me.
HAM and DANIEL PEGGOTTY start to run over to Daniel's CART.
(calling back to DAVID) Send word if that louse's mother knows where they've gone.
They jump on the cart and set off.
EXT. LONDON COACHING INN - DAY
DAVID, soaked, arrives with the tattier coaches. Unties the ropes that held him in place. Climbs down from the roof.
Cheap seats in this weather? Did
you pull your hat down tight?
Yes. As you can see, it really helped.
EXT. MRS STEERFORTH'S HOUSE - DAY
DAVID walks up to the door, and knocks.
INT. MRS STEERFORTH'S HOUSE - STAIRCASE - DAY
DAVID and LITTIMER. A silent, long walk up a grand staircase. DAVID tries to make conversation.
Uh... stairs.
LITTIMER ignores him.
INT. MRS STEERFORTH'S HOUSE - DRAWING ROOM - DAY
DAVID with MRS STEERFORTH, in an armchair reading the letter. LITTIMER lays out a table for tea.
"A lady!" She is far below him. A
She shoots DAVID a poisonous look.
Any more than a bottling boy can become a gentleman that way...
MRS STEERFORTH gestures LITTIMER over, not really listening to DAVID. She hands LITTIMER the letter.
All I have chosen to ignore in your son of snobbery and an unyielding, wilful spirit, I see in you madam.
She's focussed on DAVID again.
Do you see this?
Tea.
Each Wednesday James joins me here for tea, and this tea will not be removed from the table until he returns. That will be his welcome.
LITTIMER, off MRS STEERFORTH's signal, stands over DAVID, who gets up.
Well, he will find it stale and cold. (to LITTIMER)
LITTIMER gives DAVID the letter. He strides off.
(after him) But as for her - if there was any word of comfort that would be a solace to her in her dying hour, and only I possessed it...
INT. MRS STEERFORTH'S HOUSE - STAIRCASE - DAY
DAVID comes down the stairs, followed by LITTIMER. He speeds up a bit when he hears:
(shouting after him) ...I wouldn't part with it for life itself!
EXT. LONDON STREETS - DAY.
DAVID walks home. Even more HOMELESS FOLK, more desperate poverty. A COACH passes.
(shouts as he passes) Looking for a lift, sir?
(dejected)
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - UPSTAIRS SIDE ROOM - DAY 173
DORA and DAVID are side by side on a sofa, with JIP.
I think I know what you are about to say. Will you pose your question in Jip's voice?
I don't think that's appropriate.
You have no reason to worry about my answer.
I need to tell you: I have no money.
I don't fully understand. I'm poor.
No matter! What need have we of money when I have my singing, and you have...the tremendous thing you do...
(cuts in) Proctor.
Although, Jip must have a mutton chop every day at twelve, or he'll die...
Precisely - so how, my love, will we get meat?
Silly - I'll ask the butcher. We shall live in a pretty cottage with a lovely cook and be very happy.
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - DAY
DORA stands with DAVID. She holds a BABY. Behind them a sourfaced COOK in an apron. JIP barks and barks, the BABY cries.
We've no food.
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - UPSTAIRS SIDE ROOM - DAY 175
Back with DAVID and DORA.
I won't let Jip suffer. Nor us. I'll work extremely hard...
Why should you work?
Because...the meat? And so on. How should we live without working?

So you are to be a labourer now, you bad boy? Balancing on a plank all day with a wheelbarrow? It's all nonsense! (kissing DAVID) So - my answer is yes! I will marry you, Doady. Let us go to find Papa!
(looking dazed) Let's do that, for I am so happy!
She pulls a confused DAVID out of the room...
INT. SPENLOW & JORKINS OFFICES - STAIRWELL - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
...and down the stairs. MR SPENLOW peeks round a door. Did he...?
YES HE DID!!
A massive "HOORAY!" and the room fills with people - DORA's FAMILY, GIRLFRIENDS, SERVANTS, CLERKS, all cheering, shaking a stunned DAVID by the hand. MR SPENLOW Congratulations! Dora and Trotwood!
EXT. LONDON STREETS - DAY
DAVID in a busy street with MR DICK and his kite.
You'll have a happy lifetime together. Lifetime?
Sixty years at least! Barring... accidents.
He mimes an axe blow to the back of the neck.
But then of course you're not a king. Are you?
No, I'm not.
I mean, more particularly, you're not...
King Charles the First.
So you'll probably be fine. (a beat) The street is too full with people to get a good run with my kite.
At a road junction, MR DICK goes to carry straight on.
Ah - can't go that way Mr Dick. The gentleman who makes my waistcoats is up there and I haven't been able to fully clear my debt with him.
They turn the corner, and DAVID trips over some feet. It's MR MICAWBER, half asleep in a doorway. He looks awful, malnourished.
(shaking his hand) Mr Micawber!
(trying to look upbeat) My dear young friend! And, I believe, Mr Dixon?
Are you...well?
Never better!
He remains on the ground. DAVID notices MICAWBER's pocket square is what remains of the distinctive curtains.
And Mrs Micawber...?
In even finer fettle than myself. (through DICK's legs) There she is! With our happy tribe of dependents.
He points to MRS MICAWBER and their KIDS, who wave from a doorway further down the street, by some fruit boxes. MR MICAWBER pulls himself up slightly, now lounging like a Roman.
Do you live on the streets now?
We do currently exist primarily al fresco, with all the advantages that entails.
MR DICK gets down beside MICAWBER, lounging next to him.
Outside is so much better than

inside. Every meal is a picnic. DAVID notices that the HOMELESS MAN whom we saw take DAVID's bag is now lying in the doorway along from MICAWBER. He is covered in a filthy quilt made of DAVID's fancy waistcoats.
David. Might you help me?
(crouches down) What assistance do you need?
I popped my concertina with the Floral Street pawnbroker. I'd like it back, but he knows me and he'll charge much more than the six bob it's worth. I require a surrogate.
DAVID and MR MICAWBER both turn to MR DICK, who's happily eating a tangerine.
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - LIVING ROOM - DAY
BETSEY is with DORA and JIP in David's tiny new flat. It's been subdivided, with cloths and one of BETSEY's dresses hanging for privacy. BETSEY's VASE taking up too much room.
(looking around) I preferred, I think, the larger apartment.
We were sadly not able to express a preference.
I'm sure it's only so small because Doady is saving for a castle!
Is he really, do you think?
When we're married I should like lots of children. Five. Or ten. Or twenty - is that even possible?
If one is disciplined. Or a frog.
Doady likes children.
Doady? (re JIP) Is that this little fellow?
No, this is Jip.
Jip. Chip.
DORA BETSEY TROTWOOD
Jip. (doing JIP's voice) "Doady is my name for David." Trotwood. Doady. Trotwood.
DORA BETSEY TROTWOOD
A beat of confused silence.
Will the lady come in soon with the tea?
I will make some tea. The lady does not exist.
BETSEY gets up to make tea.
I'm sorry to hear that. Will she ever?
Bless me, you're very young.
I am. Very.
BETSEY sits down again. Leans in. Last roll of the dice.
Dora: when I was your age I married an unsuitable man and lived to bitterly regret it. We were too young, and simply incompatible.
Half a beat.
I'm so sorry you found the wrong man, but it makes me doubly happy that I've found the right one dear Doady!
(gives up) I'm very glad for you both.
INT. PAWNBROKER'S - DAY
Betsey's WINDCHIMES are in the window, with the CONCERTINA. DICK is with the PAWNBROKER. He's ready to haggle. How much for the concertina?
What if I said six shillings?
No, but I can let you have a fishing rod for six shillings.
EXT. LONDON STREET - ALLEY BESIDE PAWNBROKER'S - DAY
MR DICK stands with DAVID and MICAWBER, with a fishing rod.
No, that's not what we need.
INT. PAWNBROKER'S - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
MR DICK and the PAWNBROKER. MR DICK holds the fishing rod.
Alright, you can have the damned squeezebox. For eleven shillings. Hang on - no - twelve shillings! Haha! Thirteen! A pound!
MR DICK grabs the concertina, runs out of the shop.
EXT. LONDON STREET - OPPOSITE PAWNBROKER'S - DAY (CONTINUOUS)
MR DICK rushes out, concertina in one hand, kite in the other. As he runs the concertina wheezes chords in and out.
Half a beat as DAVID and MICAWBER take it in, then they too run, chased by the PAWNBROKER. Wide as they run, and the kite lifts in the air. DICK grinning.
EXT. LONDON STREET - DAY
DAVID, MR DICK and MICAWBER sit in the MICAWBER FAMILY's doorway, laughing. MICAWBER has his concertina.
If anything, it plays sweeter than
What an adventure! Like something from a book!
DAVID spies PEGGOTTY, with a big basket of crabs, lobsters and herring. She looks sadder and wearier. He jumps up. Peggotty!
She looks over, immediately brightens.
Davy! My precious potato!
DAVID runs to meet her and they embrace. PEGGOTTY looks across at MR DICK and MR MICAWBER, sitting on the street.
Davy, when you said you had to move to smaller premises...?
Don't worry, I don't live on the street. I'm not yet that desperate. (remembers Micawber) ...or unlucky. What brings you to London?
Dan and Ham asked me to come...
EXT. COUNTRY ROAD - DAY
MONTAGE: HAM and DANIEL PEGGOTTY. Their horse and cart relentlessly travelling, like David's walk to Dover. Travelling along a COUNTRY ROAD.
They've been searching all this time for Emily.
EXT. FLAT NORFOLK COUNTRY SIDE - DAY
Smaller in the frame, against a vast, wide, flat LANDSCAPE.
Mostly it's been like us playing
blind man's buff when you was tiny darkness and confusion, everything
EXT. LONDON - CROWDED BRIDGE - DAY
With HAM and DANIEL PEGGOTTY as they cross a busy BRIDGE. Then: Tiny in frame crossing the bridge into London. Cranes nod over the city.
Ham even sailed to France when he heard she and...that man had been seen. But they've narrowed it down now to some streets to the east. Blindfold's coming off, Davy.
EXT. LONDON STREET - DAY
PEGGOTTY, DAVID, MR MICAWBER, MR DICK.
An arduous expedition. I doff what remains of my hat to them.
You need to love those folk who help you out, and help out the ones you love. That's a Peggotty Proverb.
You're a most charitable woman.
A pregnant pause. The slightest of looks from MICAWBER to DAVID.
Mr Micawber...
I'd love to.
Would you - and please don't feel under any obligation to say yes MR MICAWBER Understood...
...but would you and your family, temporarily, like to stay, for a very short time, with me? You can say no.
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - LIVING ROOM - DAY
Later. DAVID, DICK, BETSEY, PEGGOTTY and the MICAWBERS, crammed in. MICAWBER plays his concertina. It's awful.
And you've never given a professional recital?
A perceptive question! I have sadly yet to be seriously approached.
PEGGOTTY has the basket of seafood open.
Where shall I put all these?
Thank you, but despite our losses we are all adequately fed.
Are we? (off BETSEY's look) Oh my word we are. The MICAWBER KIDS are running around.
(shouting to the KIDS) Leave that kite! Right - who needs to answer the call of nature?
MR DICK goes to put his hand up, then thinks better of it.
(to the KIDS) No volunteers? Then I'll take three of you at random...
She grabs some KIDS. DAVID looks around at his cramped flat. He's pained by the chaos he sees, and what has happened to his life. BETSEY and MR DICK clock his distress.
You really should write a story about our adventure at the pawn shop, Trotwood. It would make a cracking tale.
What adventure at the pawn shop?
There was no adventure at the pawn shop.
Yes, I should try to write...
No - no 'should'. No 'try'. You are a writer Trot.
You're a great one for surprising words. I understand them of course, but they surprise my brother.
We unpacked all your slips of paper while you were in Yarmouth. All your ideas and characters...
No - you didn't use them for kindling?
MR DICK produces a little kite-shaped BOOK, with DAVID's slips of paper carefully sewn together.
Never. We made them into a book. You really have a talent to...
MR DICK is interrupted by MICAWBER learning a new concertina tune, as MRS MICAWBER yells...
Next batch of children for the
chamber pot! Come on! Chop-chop! DAVID looks at the book. Incredibly touched by the gesture.
EXT. LONDON - EXCHANGE ALLEY - EVENING Establisher of David's second lodgings. A few HOMELESS PEOPLE
on the street outside.
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - SMALL DRESSING ROOM - EVENING

DAVID has set up a desk. Surrounded by scraps of paper. Outside we hear BETSEY, MR DICK, but mostly the MICAWBERS. We stay on DAVID looking into a mirror. The room is dark behind his reflection. DAVID trying out facial expressions, mouths words, eyes sparkling. He becomes MRS STEERFORTH.
This tea will not be removed from

the table until he returns. MRS STEERFORTH herself now appears, beside a tea table covered in cobwebs, wearing a cobwebbed dress. The room now matches the colour of her home. She says the words simultaneously.
This tea will not be removed from
the table until he returns. With her now is YOUNG DAVID, scared. MRS STEERFORTH touches her heart.
What do I touch? Your heart.
Broken! They shall lay me on this table when I am dead!
What do I touch?
Your heart.
Broken! They shall lay me on this table when I am dead!
A light on his younger self in a partial bottling-factory set, but dirtier and more ragged and begging for food:
I'm desperate with hunger. I want more sir! Please, sir...
Then STEERFORTH appears, in another area, looking slightly different:
I wish to God I had been guided to a far, far better fate... Then a light on Murdstone, in yet another area:
What I want is Facts. No boathouses! Facts! Facts alone are wanted in life.
Then MICAWBER is conjured brandishing an IOU.
As good a promissory note as any issued from Threadneedle Street! A knock on the door. A head comes round. It's the real
MICAWBER. David embarrassed, like he's been caught. Checks
Cup of tea? Or shall I leave you be with your pen? Any silence after I finish speaking I'll take as a sign to leave you be. (short silence) I'll...
Suddenly a loud knocking on the front door.
Bailiffs! Hide the spoons!
Trot! It's Agnes.
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - LIVING ROOM - (CONTINUOUS) 191

MICAWBERS, MR DICK, PEGGOTTY, BETSEY. [MRS MICAWBER sings Little Jimmy Murphy.] The door opens - AGNES carries Uriah's BRIEFCASE in one arm, a MICAWBER CHILD in the other. She arrives with purpose but is taken aback by the cramped scene.
The door was open - is this...?
(taking the CHILD) That's one of ours I think, yes.
Goodness - so many people. Will the floor hold up?
MR MICAWBER stands, bows. PEGGOTTY stands, curtseys. AGNES smiles back at both but she wants to talk to BETSEY.
Miss Trotwood, do you have... (sees MR DICK) Oh! Good morning Mr Dick.
Good morning Agnes! Please do come in, join the choir.
Miss Trotwood, do you have a letter bearing my father's signature?
Yes, I believe I do.
Searches a box/bag as DAVID enters from his writing room.
Agnes! Is something wrong?
Something's about to be made right.
DAVID crosses to her, BETSEY's there with the letter, PEGGOTTY, the MICAWBERS, MR DICK all close around Agnes.
(re briefcase) Uriah Heep's fate is in here. But I need your help. Gather around.
A beat. They're basically already gathered around. A slight shuffle from MR DICK to show willing.
EXT. WICKFIELD & HEEP OFFICES - DAY
Establisher of the offices. The sign outside is now the one we saw in scene 133 - it reads 'WICKFIELD & HEEP'.
INT. WICKFIELD & HEEP OFFICES - EVENING
URIAH works at his desk. A knock, DAVID and AGNES enter. DAVID has his coat over his arm. A brand new plaque is propped up on the table: 'HEEP & CO'.
Mr Copperfield and Miss Wickfield. Two fields. Neither laying fallow, I hope?
(to DAVID) Clever. Tiresome.
Yes, I was being polite.
You are not busy, Uriah?
(suspicious) Mr Heep is very busy. Doing the work of two men(pointedly, at AGNES) -sadly.
Well, we are here, Mr Heeeeep, to speak to you about Miss Trotwood's investments. Through the door now comes BETSEY.
More people - it's a party!
WICKFIELD and MR DICK enter.
This doesn't feel like a party.
Should I make us a bowl of punch? We need a lemon - Miss Trotwood, you look like you're sucking one.
If I had a lemon, Heep, I'd squirt the juice in your eyes. You've embezzled funds from this firm.
Slander! Who else wishes to defame me?
MICAWBER enters, with PEGGOTTY.
I do! I put it to you that - for your own pecuniary aggrandisement you falsified documents to mystify an individual whom I will designate in code as 'Mr W'.
...Maybe.
MR MICAWBER
There's no need for the code, Mr Micawber.
Prove it. You can't.
To prove it we would need access to certain documents...
But Agnes - wherever might we find such documents?
I believe they used to be in that bureau.
Used to be.
URIAH gets up and rushes to a bureau in the corner, unlocks it, looks for his briefcase. Roots around, panicky. Nothing.
(trying to appear calm) All you've proved is that you're thieves. You stole those documents.
Stole? Can Mr Wickfield's daughter not tidy up her father's papers?
They were in a locked drawer!
I'm a very enthusiastic tidier.
(re BETSEY) You stole this lady's house you greasy stain!
From under the coat he is carrying, DAVID produces URIAH'S DISTINCTIVE BRIEFCASE.
Mr Dick, what do you think?
DAVID theatrically takes a signature page from the briefcase, hands it to MR DICK. BETSEY does the same with the letter she found. Everyone crowds around DICK. He turns them upside down.
Swans?
(looking out the window) Where?
(is he going to blow it?) Swans?
(nods sagely, but not a clue) Swans.

I was studying these last night. (pause, as if that was it) Oh yes. When Mr Wickfield signs his name, the 'W' looks like a swan. But when Mr Heep mimics the signature, his 'W' is more like a church bell. Or an upturned hip bath.
A cheer from the room. The attention off him, URIAH moves to AGNES, gradually making his way towards the door.
Well done, Mr Dick!
Mr Dick cracks it again!
You are the source of all this calamity, Heep? A thousand curses on you. I take that back. A thousand and four!
MRS HEEP comes in.
(close to AGNES) Agnes, if you've any love for your babbling father you'd better leave this gang and marry me. I'll ruin him if you don't. The old ass will end his own life, I guarantee it!
Ury, make terms! Be humble, my boy.
(making for the door) No. No more pulling off our caps mother, making bows, knowing our place and abasing ourselves before our betters. No more of that.
PEGGOTTY and MICAWBER block the door. BETSEY runs over to URIAH, grabs him by the lapels.
You know what I want?
A strait-jacket? A wig?
My property.

I ain't got it. You and your kind have always hated me and mine. Kept us down. And who are you? A fine set of people. You, Copperfield, were pure scum before anyone had charity on you. And you, Miss T: you're a grim old prospect, no wonder your old man abandoned you.
BETSEY slaps URIAH. Then URIAH slaps BETSEY. Then BETSEY slaps URIAH. Then URIAH goes to slap BETSEY again but DAVID floors him with a punch.
Now stove his head in with a cake!
MRS HEEP goes to tend to URIAH.
(over unconscious URIAH) Approach us again and, if your head is human, I'll break it.
And in case it wasn't clear, you're dismissed. With immediate effect!
I expect to hear you went to a dentist on Monday to have a tooth out. And I hope it's a double one.
(recovering) You were always a puppy with a proud stomach. Riding on the coattails of that vile creature who called you Daisy.
His name is Trotwood. Davy!
At that moment DORA appears from nowhere with JIP. Doady!
I'm David Copperfield. And a constable is already on his way to take you to a magistrate, Heep.
Make sure you get put away in Pentonville, Ury. Not Millbank. You get accountants and doctors in Pentonville, it's a lovely prison.
I forgive you Mr Copperfield. I forgive all of you.
It is not for you to forgive anyone, Mr Heep. Isn't that right, Jip? (Jip voice) Yes it is.
Why is she here?
There's no reason for me to be there.
INT. DAVID'S SECOND LODGINGS - SMALL DRESSING ROOM - DAY 194
David's writing room. DORA standing, reading the early pages of a manuscript by DAVID. No JIP.
